tools.description: Access repositories, issues, and pull requests. Required for some features such as Codex

tools.name: _create_issue
tools.description: Create a GitHub issue. Returns a normalized issue snapshot, not GitHub's raw REST payload. Docs: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/issues/issues?apiVersion=2022-11-28#create-an-issue. This tool is part of plugin `GitHub`.

assignees.description: Optional GitHub usernames to assign when creating the issue.

body.description: Optional Markdown body for the issue.

labels.description: Optional labels to apply when creating the issue.

milestone.description: Optional milestone number to associate with the issue.

repository_full_name.description: Repository in `owner/name` form, such as `openai/openai`. This maps to GitHub REST `owner` and `repo` path parameters: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/repos#get-a-repository

tools.name: _add_comment_to_issue
tools.description: Create a top-level PR Conversation comment (Issue comment). This tool is part of plugin `GitHub`.

comment.description: Top-level comment body to add to the issue thread.

pr_number.description: Pull request number in the repository.

repo_full_name.description: Repository in `owner/name` form, such as `openai/openai`. This maps to GitHub REST `owner` and `repo` path parameters: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/repos#get-a-repository

tools.name: _create_blob
tools.description: Create a blob in the repository and return its SHA. This tool is part of plugin `GitHub`.

encoding.description: One of utf-8 or base64. Default is utf-8.

repository_full_name.description: Repository in `owner/name` form, such as `openai/openai`. This maps to GitHub REST `owner` and `repo` path parameters: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/repos#get-a-repository

tools.name: _create_tree

repository_full_name.description: Repository in `owner/name` form, such as `openai/openai`. This maps to GitHub REST `owner` and `repo` path parameters: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/repos#get-a-repository

tree_elements.description: Tree entries to include in the new tree object.

tools.name: _create_file
tools.description: Create a UTF-8 text file through GitHub's contents API. Returns only the resulting commit SHA, not GitHub's full content/commit payload. Docs: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/contents?apiVersion=2022-11-28#create-or-update-file-contents. This tool is part of plugin `GitHub`.

sha.description: Existing commit SHA to use as the new branch's starting point. Provide exactly one of `sha` or `base_ref`.

tools.name: _create_pull_request
tools.description: Open a pull request in the repository. Returns the connector's normalized PR snapshot, not the full REST response payload. Docs: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/pulls/pulls?apiVersion=2022-11-28#create-a-pull-request. This tool is part of plugin `GitHub`.

base.description: GitHub REST `base` branch that the pull request targets.

base_branch.description: Compatibility alias for `base`, the target branch for the pull request.

body.description: Pull request description or summary. GitHub allows omitting this field.

draft.description: Create the pull request as a draft.

head.description: GitHub REST `head` branch containing the proposed changes.

head_branch.description: Compatibility alias for `head`, the branch containing the proposed changes.

head_repo.description: Repository where the head branch lives. Required by GitHub for some same-organization cross-repository pull requests.

issue.description: Existing issue number to convert into a pull request.

maintainer_can_modify.description: Whether maintainers may modify the pull request branch.

repository_full_name.description: Repository in `owner/name` form, such as `openai/openai`. This maps to GitHub REST `owner` and `repo` path parameters: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/repos#get-a-repository

title.description: Title for the new pull request. Required unless `issue` is supplied.

{"repository_full_name":"EntityProcess/agentv-private","title":"AgentV GitHub issue triage: Beads coverage and work recommendations","body":"## Context

Reviewed the newest open GitHub issues in `EntityProcess/agentv` against local Beads coverage and AgentV repository guidelines on 2026-06-18.

Scope checked:
- Recent open GitHub issues, focused on the newest 25 by creation date.
- Local Beads coverage in the AgentV checkout, read-only.
- Alignment with AgentV principles: lightweight core, plugin extensibility, composition before new primitives, YAGNI, snake_case wire boundaries, and AI-first docs/skills.

No Beads were created during the review.

## Already covered

| GitHub issue | Bead coverage | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| EntityProcess/agentv#1392 Harbor boundary | `av-2o8` and children | Covered and correctly deferred. Do not work now. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1388 AgentV -> Opik | `av-vwa.15`, `av-vwa.15.2`, `av-vwa.16.*`, `av-bv4.6` | Covered. Work only through identity/projection dependencies first. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1385 run comparison UX | `av-2s7.10` | Covered and aligned. Worth working. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1281-#1287 Phoenix batch | partial: `av-vwa.6` | Do not create six new Beads now. Keep Phoenix at adapter/OTLP boundary unless reprioritized. |

## Recommended Beads to create

| GitHub issue | Alignment | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| EntityProcess/agentv#1391 target hooks vs workspace hooks | Strong. Schema/docs parity, no new target repo knobs, fits YAGNI. | Create focused core/docs bead and work soon. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1152 `llm_grader_results/` missing | Strong. Small bug, prevents silent zero-score regression. | Create bug bead and work soon. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1153 grader threshold docs | Strong. Clarifies an existing contract. | Create docs/behavior bead and pair with #1152. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1156 PR-yield spike | Strong. Prevents overbuilding #1159. | Create research/spike bead first. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1157 provenance block | Mostly aligned: optional, snake_case wire metadata, W3C-ish provenance. | Create schema bead only if pursuing eval-writer mining. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1158 per-test compare gate | Strong, but breaking default behavior needs release note/migration care. | Create core/CLI bead and work after scope review. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1204 env interpolation pre-parse | Plausible, but risky boundary change. | Create design/refactor bead; do not jump straight to implementation. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1259 Studio scaling | Real performance issue, but issue is over-broad. | Create narrow P1/P2 beads only; defer zero-config same-repo mode. |

## Do not create Beads yet

| GitHub issue | Reason |
|---|---|
| EntityProcess/agentv#1368 SIGTERM checkpointing | Real problem, but proposed GitHub-branch checkpoint/auto-restore is overbuilt. Create only a smaller bead if scoped to graceful shutdown plus resumable params. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1263 `agentv watch` | Expands AgentV into non-eval live observability. Keep as RFC until concrete demand. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1159 eval-writer PR/issue sources | Good placement in skills, but blocked by #1156/#1157. Create after spike proves yield. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1160 `agentv history` | Lightweight CLI wrapper, but depends on #1158 baseline workflow. Later. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1140 eval portability validator | Useful idea, but `--fix`/inlining can grow quickly. Revisit as diagnostics-only first. |
| EntityProcess/agentv#1139 public eval tracker | Product/ops initiative, not core framework work. Use existing primitives/Harbor/target hooks; no core Bead now. |

## Recommended sequence

1. Create and work quick correctness/docs fixes: #1152 and #1153.
2. Create and work #1391 target/workspace hook parity.
3. Create #1156 as a research spike before committing to PR/issue-mined eval generation.
4. If #1156 supports the direction, create #1157 provenance and then revisit #1159.
5. Create #1158 only with explicit migration/release-note scope.
6. Split #1259 into narrow P1/P2 performance beads, avoiding the larger zero-config same-repo mode until needed.

## Local state observed during triage

- AgentV checkout was clean except for an unrelated untracked file at `examples/showcase/export-screening/evals/dataset.eval.baseline.ci_check.json` during the initial review.
- No repo-local Beads, code, or tracker files were modified by the triage review."}

content.text: agentv watch - i don't understand the purpose of this. seems like that's what exporting to opik would achieve

payload.message: agentv watch - i don't understand the purpose of this. seems like that's what exporting to opik would achieve

I agree with your read.

`agentv watch` only has a distinct purpose if AgentV wants to observe a free-form agent run that is **not an eval**: no test case, no assertions, no result bundle, just “run Claude/Codex and stream spans somewhere live.”

That is a different product surface from AgentV’s core job:

- AgentV eval run: structured inputs, targets, graders, artifacts, gates.
- Opik export: project completed AgentV runs/traces/scores into an observability backend.
- `agentv watch`: ad hoc live tracing for arbitrary agent invocations.

For AgentV’s current guidelines, `watch` looks like product expansion, not a primitive we need. If the user goal is “see traces in Opik,” then `agentv eval` with trace/export support plus `agentv export opik` should cover it better because AgentV remains the runner/gate/source of truth.

I’d mark #1263 as deferred/RFC, or reframe it as docs/examples for “stream/export AgentV eval traces to Opik.” I would not create a Bead for `agentv watch` unless there is concrete demand for non-eval live observability.

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/results.mdx:22:| `results combine` | Combine partial local run workspaces into a new local run workspace |
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/results.mdx:23:| `results delete` | Delete one or more local run workspaces |

Keep those generated artifacts with the run when sharing or auditing results.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/wip-checkpoints.mdx:10:They are **not** a second results mode. They reuse the existing run workspace format and the configured git-backed results repository.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/wip-checkpoints.mdx:19:If no results repo is configured, or auto-push is disabled, `agentv eval` still writes the local run workspace and publishes completed runs to the configured local results branch, but does not create WIP branches.

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:95:In Recent Runs, select local completed runs to combine partial runs or delete stale run workspaces. Combine creates a new local run workspace and leaves the source runs in place. Delete removes the selected local run workspace directory, including sidecars such as `tags.json`; remote runs are read-only.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:147:Click any row's **Tags** cell to tag a run after the fact. Each run can carry multiple free-form tags (max 20, up to 60 characters each); local tags are stored in a `tags.json` sidecar next to `index.jsonl` in the run workspace, so they're mutable, non-destructive, and won't touch your eval YAML or run manifest. The chip editor supports Enter/comma to commit a new tag, Backspace to remove the last chip, and **Clear all** to remove every tag (deletes the sidecar).
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:348:There is no separate `agentv results remote status` or `agentv results remote sync` command. The `agentv results` CLI stays focused on local run workspaces; manual remote exchange is Dashboard/API-only, with eval auto-export covering the common CI/publisher path.

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:2:title: Workspace Pool
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:3:description: Reuse materialized workspaces across eval runs with fingerprint-based pooling, eliminating repeated clone and checkout costs.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:8:Workspace pooling keeps materialized workspaces on disk between eval runs. Instead of cloning repos and checking out files every time, pooled workspaces reset in-place — typically reducing setup from minutes to seconds for large repositories.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:10:**Pooling is enabled by default** for shared workspaces that define `repos`. No extra flags needed.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:14:AgentV computes a **SHA-256 fingerprint** of your repo materialization inputs (repo identity, checkout ref, sparse paths, and ancestor offset) and stores the materialized workspace in a persistent slot:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:17:~/.agentv/workspace-pool/
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:20:    slot-0/             # complete workspace (template files + repos)
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:43:agentv eval evals/my-eval.yaml --workspace-mode temp
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:46:### YAML workspace mode
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:49:workspace:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:57:`workspace.mode` controls materialization behavior directly (`pooled`, `temp`, or `static`).
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:63:For strict reset that also removes `.gitignore`d files, use the `--workspace-clean full` CLI flag:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:66:agentv eval evals/my-eval.yaml --workspace-clean full
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:76:Eval files that produce the **same fingerprint** share the same pool. The fingerprint is computed from the resolved workspace configuration, not the file path — so two eval files with identical workspace configs automatically reuse the same pool slots.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:78:The most reliable way to ensure shared pools is to use an [external workspace config file](#external-workspace-config):
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:82:workspace: ../workspace.yaml
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:88:workspace: ../workspace.yaml

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:96:template: ./workspace-template
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:112:Acquisition choices are excluded. A run that acquires `https://github.com/org/my-repo.git` from a registered project, a configured mirror, the AgentV mirror cache, or the remote URL still maps to the same pool if the declared repo identity and checkout inputs are the same. See [Workspace Architecture](/docs/guides/workspace-architecture/#acquisition-resolver) for the resolver order.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:140:If you change the workspace config (e.g., update a repo URL or checkout ref), the computed fingerprint changes. AgentV detects this drift by comparing the stored `metadata.json` fingerprint against the newly computed one:

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:162:## External workspace config
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:164:Instead of duplicating workspace configuration across eval files, you can reference an external YAML file:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:167:workspace: ./path/to/workspace.yaml
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:170:The external file should contain the workspace config object directly, not a nested `workspace:` key.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:172:The path is resolved relative to the eval file's directory. Relative paths **inside** the workspace file (template paths, hook `cwd` values, and repo paths) resolve from the workspace file's own directory.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:174:This pattern is especially valuable with pooling: a single `workspace.yaml` guarantees all eval files that reference it produce the same fingerprint and share the same pool.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:176:## Static workspaces (`mode: static`)
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:178:For workspaces you manage outside AgentV, use static mode:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:181:agentv eval evals/my-eval.yaml --workspace-mode static --workspace-path /path/to/my-workspace
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:184:**Auto-materialisation:** When `workspace.path` points to an empty or missing directory, AgentV automatically copies the template and clones repos into it. If the directory already exists and is populated, AgentV checks each repo individually — existing repos are reused as-is, and only missing repos are cloned. This makes static mode convenient for both first-run bootstrap and incremental setup.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:186:AgentV never deletes a user-provided workspace. Lifecycle hooks still execute (unless `hooks.enabled: false`). This is useful for local development where you already have repos checked out.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:188:**Note:** When using `--workspace-path` (CLI flag) instead of `workspace.path` (YAML), the directory is always used as-is with no auto-materialisation or repo cloning.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:190:**Precedence:** `workspace.mode` / `--workspace-mode` first, then default pooled behavior for shared repo workspaces.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-pool.mdx:194:CLI flags `--retain-on-success` / `--retain-on-failure` control temporary eval-run workspaces under `~/.agentv/workspaces/...` (non-pooled paths).

payload.message:
Yes, AgentV already does workspace setup: templates, `workspace.repos`, pooled/static workspaces, and lifecycle hooks (`before_all`, `before_each`, target hooks, etc.). The docs describe this as the eval “world” that AgentV materializes before running targets: [workspace-architecture.mdx](/home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv/apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-architecture.mdx).

But today that setup is tied to an eval run. It is not primarily a “prepare an environment, then a human drives the agent manually” product surface.

If that is the intended outcome, I would not call it `agentv watch`. I’d frame it as a workspace/session preparation feature, something like:

```bash
agentv workspace prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id bar --target codex --out /tmp/foo
```

or:

```bash
agentv eval evals/foo.eval.yaml --prepare-only --workspace-path /tmp/foo
```

Expected behavior would be: materialize repos/template, run workspace + target setup hooks, print the workspace path and suggested agent command, then stop before running graders.

Opik export is still the right answer for completed AgentV eval observability. It would not automatically capture a human manually using an agent in that prepared workspace unless AgentV either launches the agent session or imports its transcript afterward. So there are two separate ideas:

- **Eval observability:** run AgentV evals, export traces/results to Opik.
- **Human-ready harness setup:** prepare the same workspace/harness state for manual use.

The second may be useful, but it should build on workspace primitives, not become a broad `watch` command.

content.text: so then what would be  the command surface. i think agentv prepare would be a better command since eval would indicate it's an evaluation? is this called human evals

payload.message: so then what would be  the command surface. i think agentv prepare would be a better command since eval would indicate it's an evaluation? is this called human evals

`agentv prepare` is the better name if the purpose is “make the harness environment ready for a human or external agent.” I would avoid putting this under `agentv eval` because that implies AgentV is actually running and grading an evaluation.

I also would not call this “human evals.” In eval tooling, that usually means **humans are judging/scoring outputs**. Here the human is the **operator** using a prepared AgentV workspace. Better names: “manual run,” “prepared session,” or “harness session.”

A minimal command surface could be:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex
```

Output:

```text
Workspace ready: /home/me/.agentv/prepared/foo/case-1/codex
Target: codex
Prepared through: workspace before_all -> target before_all -> workspace before_each -> target before_each

Next:
  cd /home/me/.agentv/prepared/foo/case-1/codex
  codex
```

Useful flags:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --out /tmp/agentv-case-1
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target claude --json
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --keep
```

Semantics should be explicit:

- Materialize `workspace.template` and `workspace.repos`.
- Run setup hooks only: `workspace before_all`, `target before_all`, `workspace before_each`, `target before_each`.
- Do **not** run the agent.
- Do **not** grade.
- Do **not** call it a completed eval run.
- Write a small manifest like `agentv-prepare.json` so the workspace is auditable.

If we later want to score what happened manually, that should be a separate command:

```bash
agentv grade-prepared /tmp/agentv-case-1 --eval evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1
```

or maybe:

```bash
agentv eval import-manual /tmp/agentv-case-1 ...
```

But v1 should probably stop at `agentv prepare`. That keeps it a workspace primitive, not a new observability product or a hidden eval mode.

content.text: so then should agentv evaluate using the transcript / session. would it need to import the session

payload.message: so then should agentv evaluate using the transcript / session. would it need to import the session

Yes. If a human uses a prepared workspace, AgentV should evaluate from two inputs afterward:

1. The final workspace state: file diffs, test results, code-graders, functional checks.
2. The agent transcript/session, if trajectory/tool behavior matters.

So the clean model is:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --out /tmp/agentv-case-1
# human runs codex/claude/etc in that workspace
agentv evaluate-session evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 \
  --workspace /tmp/agentv-case-1 \
  --transcript ~/.codex/sessions/...jsonl
```

But I’d avoid a new top-level verb if existing concepts can cover it. Better command shape may be:

```bash
agentv eval evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 \
  --workspace-mode static \
  --workspace-path /tmp/agentv-case-1 \
  --transcript ~/.codex/sessions/...jsonl \
  --skip-target
```

The question is whether `--skip-target` is too weird. It means “do not run the agent target; treat this workspace/session as the completed target output and run graders.”

Import is needed if the transcript is provider-native and not already AgentV-normalized. AgentV already has trace-evaluation Beads around normalized trajectories, transcript import, Pi JSONL import, compact logs, etc. That is the right architecture: provider-specific session logs get imported into AgentV’s normalized trajectory model; graders consume the normalized trajectory, not Codex/Claude/Pi internals.

I’d split it like this:

```bash
agentv prepare ...
agentv import session <provider-log> --format codex --out session.agentv-trace.jsonl
agentv evaluate-prepared evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 \
  --workspace /tmp/agentv-case-1 \
  --trace session.agentv-trace.jsonl
```

For v1, I’d keep it simpler:

```bash
agentv prepare ...
agentv evaluate-prepared ... --workspace ... --transcript ...
```

Internally, `evaluate-prepared` can call the same import adapter. The durable concept should be “evaluate a prepared workspace,” with transcript import as optional enrichment.

---
title: AgentV — Real-Time Observability & Evaluation Strategy
created: 2026-05-15
updated: 2026-06-04
type: synthesis
tags: [synthesis, evaluation, framework, agentic, serving, prediction]
sources: [raw/articles/vscode-copilot-monitoring-agents.md, raw/articles/llm-evaluation-observability-best-practices.md]
---

# AgentV — Real-Time Observability & Evaluation Strategy

Recommended architecture for evolving [[agentv]] from an offline eval framework into an all-in-one offline-eval + live-observability tool. Distilled from existing wiki entities ([[arize-phoenix]], [[langfuse]], [[langwatch]], [[braintrust]], [[pentagi]], [[vscode-copilot-agents]]), the OpenTelemetry GenAI semconv spec (May 2026), Aspire Dashboard docs, and the actual `packages/core/src/observability/` code in `EntityProcess/agentv`.

**Headline:** AgentV does not need to "adopt OTel" — it already has. The work left is finishing alignment with the GenAI semantic conventions, shipping a one-command local viewer recipe, and exposing the existing streaming observer as a standalone `agentv watch` surface so it isn't gated behind `agentv run`.

---

## 0. State of the union (what AgentV already ships)

`packages/core/src/observability/`:

| Component | What it does | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `OtelTraceExporter` | Wraps `@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node`; OTLP/HTTP + OTLP JSON file processors | Shipping |
| `OtelStreamingObserver` | Creates real-time `agentv.eval` root span + child `execute_tool` / `chat` spans during eval execution | Shipping (wired in `apps/cli/src/commands/eval/run-eval.ts:843`) |
| `OtlpJsonFileExporter` | Standard OTLP-JSON wire format on disk — importable by any OTel backend | Shipping |
| `OTEL_BACKEND_PRESETS` | Zero-config presets for `langfuse`, `braintrust`, `confident` (Langfuse derives endpoint from `LANGFUSE_HOST`, Braintrust auto-builds `x-bt-parent` from project env vars) | Shipping |
| W3C `traceparent` propagation | Reads `TRACEPARENT` / `TRACESTATE` from env, links AgentV spans into a caller's trace | Shipping |
| Per-span token usage | `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` / `output_tokens` / `cache_read.input_tokens` | Shipping |
| `groupTurns` flag | Wraps messages into `agentv.turn.N` spans for multi-turn evals | Shipping |
| Custom `agentv.*` attributes | `agentv.test_id`, `agentv.target`, `agentv.score`, etc. — live alongside `gen_ai.*` | Shipping |

Open tracking issues (per [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]]):

- **#298** GenAI semconv full alignment — replace remaining `agentv.*` attrs with `gen_ai.*` where a standard exists (HIGH, 2–3 days)
- **#299** per-span token usage — partially done; final pass to make sure every `chat` span emits
- **#300** session tracing plugin — agent-level OTel beyond evals (2 weeks)
- **#301** W3C `traceparent` linking — appears done in code; verify
- **#302** turn-level span grouping — appears done; verify
- **#304** OTLP JSON file + JSONL exporters — done
- **#305** real-time streaming OTel export — `OtelStreamingObserver` exists; the gap is using it outside the eval orchestrator
- **#306** lazy file-backed code-judge output for >50 KB

So the "should AgentV adopt OTel?" question is closed. The remaining design choices are: **how the existing pieces compose into a live observability product**, and **how live traces and offline eval data share one model**.

---

## 1. Landscape: OTel vs purpose-built

### Approach A — OTel-native, BYO backend

**Used by:** [[arize-phoenix]] (OpenInference layered on OTel), [[langfuse]] (SDK v4 is a thin OTel wrapper, gen_ai-compliant), [[langwatch]] (OTel-native ingest), VS Code Copilot ([[vscode-copilot-agents]]), [[pentagi]] (Go OTel + Langfuse). Datadog LLM Observability now consumes GenAI semconv natively. Honeycomb / Grafana Tempo / Aspire Dashboard ingest generic OTLP.

**The trade-off table:**

| Dimension | OTel-native | Purpose-built (proprietary SDK) |
|---|---|---|
| Lock-in | Low — point OTLP at any backend; switch endpoints, not code | High — rewrite instrumentation to switch vendors |
| Span model | Standard `gen_ai.*` semconv (still **Development**, no Stable yet — spec v1.37) | Vendor-specific; e.g. Langfuse `observation` types (10 of them), Phoenix `Annotation.kind` |
| Tooling | OTel collector, Tempo, Jaeger, Aspire Dashboard, Datadog, Honeycomb — all free | Vendor's UI only |
| Production maturity | OTel SDK itself is rock solid; `gen_ai.*` semconv is still settling — `gen_ai.system` deprecated → `gen_ai.provider.name`, all attrs still marked Development | Vendor's product is mature within its walls |
| Multi-agent trace linking | W3C `traceparent` propagates across subprocesses automatically (VS Code Copilot uses this for subagents) | Vendor-specific session IDs |
| Cost | Free at the protocol; pay for storage/UI | Pay-per-event or seat |

### Approach B — purpose-built observability SDK

[[braintrust]] (commercial SaaS — its eval+observability pipeline is the product) and the older LangSmith model. You write to their SDK; their UI is the only consumer. Faster to integrate the first time, hard to migrate later.

**Verdict:** Industry has already converged on OTel. Phoenix is BSD-3 OSS. Langfuse SDK v4 is a thin OTel wrapper. Braintrust accepts OTLP. The only remaining purpose-built holdout pattern is "wrap an OTel SDK and call it ours," which is what Langfuse, LangWatch, and Phoenix actually do.

For AgentV: **OTel is the moat, not the cage.** It lets AgentV stay aligned with [[arize-phoenix]]'s evaluation primitives, [[langfuse]]'s span model, and [[vscode-copilot-agents]]'s subagent propagation — without picking a winner.

### Sub-question: zero-config local viewer

This is the one place "purpose-built" wins a narrow battle. None of {Phoenix, Langfuse, Datadog} is a one-command local dev experience. **Aspire Dashboard** is:

```
docker run --rm -d --name aspire-dashboard \
  -p 18888:18888 -p 4317:18889 -p 4318:18890 \
  -e ASPIRE_DASHBOARD_UNSECURED_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true \
  mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspire-dashboard:latest
```

Then `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317 agentv run ...` and traces appear in the UI. Traces, metrics, logs — all three signals. No login. Already documented by VS Code as the recommended local viewer.

This belongs in AgentV's docs as the "first 5 minutes" path. Not a built-in. Not a fork. A 6-line `docker run` in the docs.

---

## 2. Should AgentV adopt OTel natively?

**Already done.** Restating the question productively: *what does "natively" mean from here?*

There are three levels of "native":

1. **Emit traces during eval execution** — done. `OtelStreamingObserver` opens `agentv.eval` on startEvalCase and emits `execute_tool` / `chat` children live.
2. **Emit traces during non-eval agent invocation** — *the actual gap.* Today AgentV only emits when running through the eval orchestrator. To be a live observability product, the same span emitter has to be reachable when the user just wants to *watch* an agent (no test case, no graders).
3. **Score traces in flight** — gradable spans during execution rather than after. Today's graders run on a completed `EvaluationResult`. To do *real-time* eval, graders need to consume the streamed span/event view.

Recommendation: **yes to all three, in that order.** (1) is done. (2) and (3) are the work.

This also stays inside the strategic posture already committed in [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] §"Out-of-scope": **don't build a hosted dashboard as a SaaS.** Export via OTel. AgentV : Braintrust :: pytest : Datadog.

---

## 3. Recommended architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          AgentV runtime                             │
│                                                                     │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────┐     │
│   │  agentv run          │         │  agentv watch (NEW)      │     │
│   │  (offline eval)      │         │  (live observability)    │     │
│   │  → graders → JSONL   │         │  → no graders, just spans│     │
│   └─────────┬────────────┘         └─────────────┬────────────┘     │
│             │                                    │                  │
│             ▼                                    ▼                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│   │     Provider adapters (Claude / Codex / Copilot     │           │
│   │     SDK / CLI / Pi) — already streaming-capable     │           │
│   └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘           │
│                         │ stream callbacks                          │
│                         ▼                                           │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│   │     OtelStreamingObserver (already exists)          │           │
│   │     emits: invoke_agent, chat, execute_tool spans   │           │
│   │     gen_ai.* attrs + W3C traceparent propagation    │           │
│   └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘           │
│                         │                                           │
│        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                          │
│        ▼                ▼                ▼                          │
│   OTLP file        OTLP/HTTP        OTLP/gRPC                       │
│   (replay)         (any backend)    (collector chain)               │
└────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┼────────────────────────┘
         │                 │                 │
         ▼                 ▼                 ▼
    .agentv/        Aspire (local)     Phoenix / Langfuse /
    traces/         dev viewer         Braintrust / Datadog /
    *.otlp.json                        Tempo / Honeycomb …
```

### Shared data model

The only data model AgentV needs is the **OTel span tree with GenAI semantic-convention attributes**:

- Root: `invoke_agent` (rename from current `agentv.eval` for non-eval flows; keep both)
- Children: `chat` (LLM calls), `execute_tool` (tool invocations), `agentv.turn.N` (turn grouping)
- Attributes: `gen_ai.operation.name`, `gen_ai.provider.name` (replacing deprecated `gen_ai.system`), `gen_ai.request.model`, `gen_ai.usage.{input,output}_tokens`, `gen_ai.tool.name`, `gen_ai.tool.call.{id,arguments,result}`
- Resource attributes: `service.name` (= `agentv` or the agent provider name, mirroring VS Code Copilot's `service.name` taxonomy), `service.version`

**Score attachment:** scores become OTel **span events** (this is what the code does already) with attributes `agentv.score.{name,value,verdict,source}`. This maps cleanly onto:

- Phoenix's `Annotation { kind: "LLM" | "CODE" | "HUMAN" }`
- Langfuse's `Score.source = "ANNOTATION" | "API" | "EVAL"`
- LangWatch's `is_guardrail` dual-use flag

Adopting score `source` provenance (see [[score-provenance]]) closes [[arize-phoenix]] and [[langfuse]] convergence gaps the wiki has already flagged.

### Why offline and online share one model

The trick to making offline-eval and live-obs the same product is **the span IS the result**.

- An offline eval produces a JSONL + an OTLP-JSON file. Same spans, written to disk.
- A live observability session produces an OTLP/HTTP stream to Phoenix/Langfuse/Aspire. Same spans, written to network.
- Re-scoring a historical run is reading the OTLP-JSON file back and running graders against it. No re-invocation, no re-cost.

This is exactly the [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]] pattern, just spelled in OTel instead of WTG's JSONL: every metric computed from one canonical event stream, online and offline aggregators share code. AgentV's existing `computeTraceSummary(output)` is the offline aggregator; the streaming observer is the online one. **They should consume the same span representation.** Today they don't quite — `computeTraceSummary` builds a `TraceSummary` from messages, while the streaming observer emits OTel spans. Unifying them on a shared OTel-shaped in-memory model is the cleanup.

---

## 4. MVP — what to implement first

Three releasable slices, smallest blast radius first. Each one closes an existing tracked issue.

### MVP-1 (1 week): finish GenAI semconv alignment and ship the Aspire docs recipe

Closes #298, finishes #299, validates #301 + #302.

- Replace `gen_ai.system = 'agentv'` with `gen_ai.provider.name = '<provider>'` (or keep both for one release; deprecate the old form). Spec is in Development but the rename is settled.
- Add `service.name = '<provider-name>'` and `gen_ai.agent.name = '<target.name>'` as resource attributes. Match the VS Code Copilot taxonomy (`copilot-chat`, `github-copilot`, `claude-code`, …) so cross-tool traces are queryable together.
- Add `REDACTED` honoring once the spec settles further.
- Docs: a 6-line `docker run mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspire-dashboard:latest` recipe at `apps/web/src/content/docs/observability/local-viewer.md`. Tested screenshot. Done.

Acceptance signal: a user can run `agentv run my.eval.yaml --export-otel` and see the trace in Aspire Dashboard in under 5 minutes.

### MVP-2 (1–2 weeks): `agentv watch` — observability outside of evals

The big architectural change. Closes #300.

- New command: `agentv watch <agent-target>` — wires the same `OtelStreamingObserver` around a free-form agent invocation, no test case required, no graders. Inputs from stdin or `--input` file; output streamed to the configured OTLP endpoint.
- Subagent propagation: set `TRACEPARENT` + `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` in the child process env so any agentv-instrumented subprocess (or anything reading W3C tracecontext, like Copilot CLI) becomes a child span automatically.
- Optionally: a `--collect` flag that writes traces to `.agentv/traces/watch-<ts>.otlp.json` for later replay.

Acceptance signal: `agentv watch claude-sdk -i prompt.md` produces a complete OTel trace tree visible in any OTLP backend. A subsequent `agentv eval <file> --replay .agentv/traces/watch-<ts>.otlp.json` re-scores it without re-running the agent.

### MVP-3 (2–3 weeks): in-flight scoring

The "real-time eval" piece. Closes #305 properly.

Two complementary paths — ship whichever fits the user's posture:

- **Tap-and-score (in-process)**: in `agentv watch`, run lightweight graders as spans close. Latency and cost graders trigger on `chat` span end. Tool-call assertion graders trigger on `execute_tool` end. Guardrail-style LLM graders run async on a sampled subset (mirrors [[langfuse]]'s `JobConfiguration { delay, samplingPct }`). Emit score results as span events on the parent — they show up in any backend automatically.
- **Forward-and-defer**: emit only; rely on Phoenix online evaluators or Langfuse jobs to score the stream. AgentV becomes the emitter; the backend is the scorer. This is cheaper for users who already run Phoenix.

The default should be Tap-and-score with the lightweight deterministic graders (latency, cost, token_usage, regex, contains, execution_metrics) and `--score-async` for LLM-based ones. This is consistent with [[langwatch]]'s `is_guardrail` dual-use boolean — every grader becomes either online (guardrail) or offline (analytical) based on a single flag.

Acceptance signal: an `agentv watch` session with `--score latency,cost,tool_trajectory` emits scores into the live trace within ~100 ms of each span closing, and they render as Phoenix `Annotation`s / Langfuse `Score`s.

### What to *not* build for MVP

- A hosted dashboard. Aspire / Phoenix / Langfuse already exist. The product-strategy in [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] is explicit about this.
- A new wire format. OTel + OTLP is the format.
- A built-in collector. The OTel Collector exists; document it.
- Auth, multi-tenancy, retention policies. That's what Phoenix/Langfuse/Datadog do.

---

## 5. Offline evals and live observability — how they coexist

The way to frame it for the docs:

> **AgentV is an agent telemetry source.** Offline evals are telemetry generated under a structured test plan with graders attached. Live observability is the same telemetry generated under whatever the user happens to be doing. The data model is identical. The difference is the input scaffolding and which graders fire when.

Concretely:

| | Offline eval (`agentv run`) | Live observability (`agentv watch`) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | YAML test cases | stdin / prompt file / piped CLI |
| Iteration | One per test case, repeated for trials | One per invocation; no loop |
| Graders | All declared graders run after the run | Lightweight graders run live; heavy ones run async or offline |
| Workspace lifecycle | `before_all` / `before_each` / `after_each` / `after_all` | None — agent runs in user's actual cwd |
| Output | JSONL + OTLP file + scores | OTLP stream (+ optional OTLP file) |
| Re-scoring | Read JSONL or replay OTLP file | Read the OTLP file from `--collect` |
| Default backend | None (file-only) | Whatever `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` says |

One CLI surface, two modes. The orchestrator code branches at the *graders-attached* / *workspace-lifecycle* level, not at the *telemetry* level.

This also gives AgentV a clean answer to the [[harness-quality-evaluation]] vision: a long-running `agentv watch --collect` over a developer's real Claude/Codex/Copilot session is exactly the input you need to build harness-quality scoring, edit-survival metrics (the VS Code Copilot `four_gram` / `no_revert` signals), and longitudinal evaluation. The MVP doesn't have to implement those — it just has to not block them.

The [[production-llm-evaluation-observability-loop]] source adds a useful productization constraint: `agentv watch` should make cost, latency, cache hits, prompt/harness version, and rollout/variant labels first-class span attributes from the start. Those fields are not just dashboard metadata; they become replay filters, CI gate inputs, and regression dimensions once production traces are fed back into offline suites.

---

## 6. Risks and counters

- **OTel `gen_ai.*` is still Development.** The deprecation of `gen_ai.system` happened in this cycle. Counter: AgentV already supports `OTEL_SEMCONV_STABILITY_OPT_IN`-shaped opt-in (just needs to be wired); keep the old `agentv.*` attributes alongside until the spec stabilizes (target: `gen_ai_latest_experimental` opt-in honored, plus a `--semconv-stable-only` flag for users who want only Stable attrs).
- **Phoenix vs Langfuse vs Braintrust default story confuses users.** Counter: do not default. The presets exist; the docs should explicitly say "if you don't have a backend, run Aspire Dashboard locally" with the docker command, and stop there.
- **Live scoring with LLM graders is expensive at watch-scale.** Counter: Langfuse's sampling pattern (`samplingPct`) — copy it. Make the default for live scoring "deterministic graders only" with an opt-in `--score-async --sample 0.1` for LLM grading.
- **Span attribute payload bloat.** VS Code Copilot's `COPILOT_OTEL_MAX_ATTRIBUTE_SIZE_CHARS` truncation knob is the precedent. AgentV's existing #306 (lazy file-backed code-judge output for >50 KB) is the same problem; one solution covers both.
- **Spec drift between AgentV's offline `TraceSummary` and the emitted OTel spans.** Counter: collapse `computeTraceSummary` into a derivation from the in-memory span tree. One source, two views.

---

## 7. Concrete next actions

1. Open issue `agentv: agentv watch — live observability surface (RFC)` referencing this document; link from #300.
2. Land MVP-1 alignment PR (rename `gen_ai.system` → `gen_ai.provider.name`, add `service.name` resource attr, ship the Aspire local-viewer docs page). Closes #298.
3. Land MVP-2 PR introducing `apps/cli/src/commands/watch/`. Reuses `OtelStreamingObserver` directly. Closes #300.
4. Open a follow-up issue for MVP-3 in-flight scoring; defer until #300 ships and we have telemetry on what users actually run under `watch`.

# Agent Harness Engineering: A Survey

New academic survey paper (under review for TMLR) by researchers from CMU, Yale, Johns Hopkins, Amazon, and others. Reviewed 170+ open-source agent projects and incorporated real production lessons from OpenAI, Anthropic, and LangChain.

## Core Thesis
**Agent performance in the real world = Model capability + Harness quality.**

For long-horizon, multi-step, production tasks, the **harness** (infrastructure wrapping the model: execution env, tools, context/memory, lifecycle/orchestration, observability, verification, governance) has become the primary bottleneck. Simple, targeted harness improvements often outperform incremental model gains.

## ETCLOVG 7-Layer Taxonomy
The paper formalizes agent harness engineering around:
- **E**xecution environment
- **T**ooling
- **C**ontext management
- **L**ifecycle / Orchestration
- **O**bservability
- **V**erification
- **G**overnance

## Relevance to OSS
- Explicitly cites [[muratcan-koylan]]'s **Agent Skills for Context Engineering** project in the Context & Memory Management section.
- Validates the shift from "build one agent" to "operate fleets of long-running agents".
- Emphasizes turning production traces into regression tests and automated harness fixes as a high-leverage practice.

## Related
- [[harness-engineering]]
- [[context-engineering]]
- [[agentic-harness-engineering]]
- [[muratcan-koylan]]

## Cross-References
- Complements existing harness work in [[harness-engineering]] and [[harness-quality-evaluation]]
- See also NexAU autonomous harness evolution in [[agentic-harness-engineering]]

# Benchmark Provenance and Workspace Patterns

Eval frameworks need to separate **source provenance**, **operational setup**, and **scoring artifacts**. When those are collapsed into one schema, the framework accumulates source-specific fields. When they are separated, [[agentv]] can stay a small execution layer while still representing benchmark-style suites through metadata, hooks, case directories, graders, and result bundles.

## Durable pattern

| Concern | Durable representation | Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Source provenance | Frozen source pins in `metadata`, source task packs, generator manifests, case IDs, row IDs, source commits | A top-level schema field for every benchmark family |
| Operational setup | Workspace repos, lifecycle hooks, Dockerfile/image/Compose templates, setup scripts that receive case metadata | Mixing informational provenance with setup fields |
| Gold data and oracles | Expected output, hidden tests, code graders, shell tests, case-directory fixtures | Treating oracle validation as a special runtime mode |
| Score provenance | Score source/kind, evaluator ID, explanation/evidence, raw assertion arrays | Anonymous scores with no judge/metric/human distinction |
| Run artifacts | Immutable result directories with logs, traces, file diffs, grader output, and workspace references | Dashboard-only state or mutable run summaries |

The practical rule for AgentV docs is: use metadata for audit context, hooks for setup, graders for verification, and generated files/templates for ceremony reduction. Add schema only for universal meaning that would otherwise be lost, such as rubric criterion operators in [[agentv-eval-authoring-extensibility]].

## Peer-framework lessons

- [[promptfoo]] maps well to prompt/provider/test matrices and JavaScript assertions. It can approximate workflow-plan or rubric checks, but workspace setup, live tool trajectories, and full AgentV code-grader payloads require custom adapters.
- [[braintrust]] maps cleanly to Data/Task/Scores and trial-count loops. Rich AgentV assertions, trace summaries, cost, duration, and file-change evidence usually become score metadata unless a deeper adapter exists.
- [[arize-phoenix]] is strongest for persisted datasets, experiments, score annotations, and OpenTelemetry traces. Its normal experiment flow assumes a Phoenix dataset/server round trip, so local YAML-style benchmark authoring needs wrapper scripts rather than core AgentV schema changes.
- [[margin-evals]], [[terminal-bench]], [[terminal-bench-2]], and [[skillsbench]] show the benchmark-runtime side: filesystem-native cases, fresh isolated environments, post-agent test staging, hidden/reference solution separation, and structured artifacts.

## Container/workspace pattern

Terminal-style benchmarks converge on fresh execution identities per task or trial, with reusable environment assets:

- Use a fresh container, workspace, or Compose project identity per case/trial.
- Allow Dockerfile builds, prebuilt images, and Compose overlays where multi-service dependencies matter.
- Cache or digest-pin images so reproducibility and runtime cost are visible.
- Stage hidden tests after agent execution when the benchmark needs to prevent direct inspection.
- Persist logs, test output, traces, artifacts, and run manifests in a structured bundle.

For AgentV, these are principles to document and template, not a mandate to import Harbor's full task schema or Margin's single-container assumptions. A minimal AgentV workspace/container primitive should remain small enough that plain lifecycle hooks and case directories still cover most benchmark authors.

## Public-safe boundary

Private parity work is useful because it exposes ceremony and lossy mappings against real corpora. The public wiki and AgentV docs should keep only the generalizable findings:

- which primitives map cleanly across frameworks
- which mappings require adapters or templates
- which AgentV gaps lose source meaning
- which peer features are useful patterns but not core schema

Do not copy private competitor artifacts, confidential task details, or organization-specific setup into public examples. Promote sanitized patterns only after hand ports prove they recur.

- [[promptfoo-vs-braintrust-vs-agentv]]
- [[agentv-vs-margin-evals]]
- [[score-provenance]]
- [[tool-trajectory-evaluation]]

title: Cassette Record / Replay
created: 2026-05-12
updated: 2026-05-12
type: concept
tags: [evaluation, framework, agentic, data-pipeline]
sources: [raw/articles/dexter-evals.md, raw/articles/vscode-copilot-monitoring-agents.md]

# Cassette Record / Replay

## Definition
A determinism strategy: during development, record every tool call and its result to a JSONL "cassette" file; in CI, replay from the cassette instead of executing the tool. Matches are by canonicalized `(tool, args)` pairs. Cassette miss = hard failure, which surfaces unintended behavior changes immediately.

## Origin / who uses it
- [[runledger]] — canonical implementation. Cassette = JSONL of tool call/result tuples with normalization rules.
- [[vibe-kanban]] — uses replay over ACP traces for deterministic UI testing.
- [[promptfoo]] — `cache: true` is a degenerate cassette (no normalization).
- [[langwatch]], [[langfuse]] — trace storage that can be reused as replay sources.
- [[adk-js]] — OpenTelemetry spans replayable via [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]].

## Why it matters
CI flakiness on agentic systems comes from two sources:
1. **API non-determinism**: temperature > 0 sampling, server-side load balancing, time-of-day model variation.
2. **External tool non-determinism**: network calls, timestamps, request_ids, ephemeral filesystem state.

Cassettes neutralize both. A passing CI run with cassettes proves "given this fixed sequence of tool results, the agent produces this fixed behavior." It does *not* prove the agent works against live tools — that requires periodic re-recording.

## Implementation snapshot
Cassette entry (JSONL):
```json
{"tool": "Read", "args": {"path": "spec.md"}, "result": {"content": "Build a TODO app..."}, "ts": "2026-05-12T10:00:00Z"}
{"tool": "Bash", "args": {"command": "npm test"}, "result": {"exit_code": 0, "stdout": "5 passing"}, "ts": "2026-05-12T10:00:03Z"}
```

Normalization rules (strip volatile fields before matching):
```yaml
cassette:
  path: cassettes/todo-app.jsonl
  normalize:
    strip_keys: [ts, request_id, trace_id]
    strip_paths: ["$.result.headers.date"]
    replace_text:
      - pattern: '/tmp/[a-z0-9]{8}/'
        with: '/tmp/STABLE/'
  on_miss: fail        # alternative: passthrough | warn
```

Canonical match logic:
```
match(actual, recorded):
  return canonicalize(actual.tool, actual.args) == canonicalize(recorded.tool, recorded.args)
```

## Open questions / variations
- **Recording mode**: live-record on first run? Periodically re-record? [[runledger]] does both via mode flags.
- **Partial replay**: replay tool A but live-call tool B. Useful when one tool is deterministic and the other is not.
- **Cassette format**: JSONL is universal; OpenTelemetry spans are richer but heavier.
- **Privacy**: cassettes capture real API responses. Treat as secrets if they contain PII.
- **Drift detection**: when does a cassette miss mean "the test is wrong" vs "the agent changed"? Hard problem.

## AgentV implication
AgentV does not yet have cassettes. Adding them would:
1. Make CI deterministic without freezing the agent's behavior space.
2. Cut CI cost (no API calls on replay).
3. Surface behavior changes loudly (cassette miss = test failure).

Sequencing: depends on [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]] being canonical. Once events are the metric source, recording them is free.

# Production LLM Evaluation and Observability Loop

## Definition

A production operating loop for LLM applications where prompts, model calls, traces, eval datasets, scores, dashboards, rollouts, and human feedback are linked by stable IDs. The core idea is that evaluation is not a separate offline ritual: the same instrumentation that powers live observability should also power regression tests, CI gates, cost controls, and post-deployment quality analysis.

The pattern generalizes the stack in `LLM_EVALUATION_AND_OBSERVABILITY_BEST_PRACTICES.md`: Langfuse or a similar backend for production traces, [[arize-phoenix]] for local trace debugging, [[ragas]] or custom metrics for semantic scoring, and CI/rollout gates to prevent bad prompt or model changes from reaching all users.

## Operating loop

1. **Version every prompt and harness input.** Prompt templates, model parameters, tool lists, retrieval configuration, safety settings, and evaluator thresholds are deployable artifacts.
2. **Instrument once at the model/client boundary.** Every generation records prompt version, model, parameters, token usage, latency, cost, cache status, trace ID, user/session metadata, and variant/experiment labels.
3. **Run fast gates before expensive judges.** Schema checks, JSON parsing, safety regexes, length bounds, and contract tests fail quickly before LLM judges or human review spend more tokens.
4. **Score semantically where deterministic checks are insufficient.** RAG faithfulness, answer relevance, structured extraction accuracy, tool-trajectory quality, and safety/compliance belong in separate score fields with provenance.
5. **Gate CI and rollout separately.** CI hard gates block known regressions; progressive rollout watches live metrics under real traffic and trips circuit breakers on latency, cost, error, or quality regressions.
6. **Feed production traces back into evals.** Failed traces, high-cost traces, human-corrected outputs, and near-miss rollout incidents become new eval cases.

## Why it matters

This loop closes a gap between offline eval frameworks and production observability platforms. Offline evals are useful but stale if they do not ingest production failures. Observability is useful but passive if traces do not become tests and gates. The durable system ties them together: every prompt/model/harness change gets evaluated before merge, watched during rollout, and mined after deployment.

The same principle applies to coding agents. A future [[agentv]] live-observability surface should not just show traces; it should collect traces that can be replayed, rescored, and turned into [[harness-quality-evaluation]] cases. That makes [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]] the shared substrate for both offline evaluation and production monitoring.

## Evaluation layers

| Layer | Examples | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Contract checks | JSON/schema validity, required fields, tool args | Fail malformed outputs cheaply |
| Fast filters | unsafe substrings, PII patterns, length and latency bounds | Block obvious failures before semantic scoring |
| Domain metrics | [[ragas]] faithfulness/relevance, extraction accuracy, task-specific rubrics | Measure task quality |
| Harness metrics | tool trajectory, exploration ratio, token/pass, delegation events | Measure how the agent used its environment |
| Human review | critical workflows, calibration sets, disputed judge calls | Correct and calibrate automated scores |
| Rollout monitors | success rate, P95 latency, cost/session, cache hit rate, error rate | Catch production regressions |

## AgentV implication

For [[agentv]], this pattern argues for one shared trace model across `agentv run` and any future `agentv watch` surface:

- Offline eval runs emit the same spans that live observability emits.
- Scores attach to spans with [[score-provenance]] so dashboards can separate code judges, LLM judges, human annotations, and deterministic metrics.
- Cost, latency, cache hit rate, and token usage are first-class evaluators, not only dashboard fields.
- CI gates and rollout gates are different consumers of the same scores: CI blocks merge, rollout blocks traffic expansion.
- Production traces can be replayed into eval suites without re-running the model when the captured event stream is complete enough.

## Open questions

- How much production trace data should be sampled into evals by default?
- Which traces need human review before becoming golden cases?
- Should cost and latency gates be absolute thresholds, regression thresholds, or both?
- How should semantic-cache hits be evaluated when they avoid a model call but may preserve stale behavior?

# Data + Task + Scores Triad

## Definition
The universal three-part decomposition of any AI evaluation: **Data** (inputs/expected outputs), **Task** (the agent or system being tested), and **Scores** (evaluator functions that grade output). Named by [[braintrust]] but implicit in every one of the 21+ eval frameworks surveyed in [[evaluation-frameworks-landscape]]. This is the load-bearing mental model that underwrites the AgentEvals open standard.

## Origin / who uses it
- [[braintrust]] — coined the canonical naming (`data`, `task`, `scores`).
- [[wtg-agent-evaluator]] — calls the same triad "test set / DUT (device under test) / metric".
- [[runledger]] — labels it "suite / agent / assertions".
- [[promptfoo]] — `tests` / `providers` / `assert`.
- [[openai-evals]] — `samples` / `completion_fn` / `eval`.
- [[inspect-ai]] — `Dataset` / `Solver` / `Scorer`.
- [[deepeval]] — `dataset` / `LLM` / `metrics`.
- [[ragas]], [[langfuse]], [[langwatch]], [[arize-phoenix]], [[trulens]] — same shape, different vocabulary.

## Why it matters
Every framework that diverges from this triad ends up reinventing it. The triad is the minimum surface area for an evaluation to be: (a) reproducible (data is fixed), (b) comparable (task is swappable), (c) measurable (scores are deterministic functions). Once a framework adopts it, cross-framework portability becomes a vocabulary mapping rather than a re-architecture.

## Implementation snapshot
AgentV maps cleanly:
```yaml
tests:                # Data (inputs + expected)
  - id: simple-task
    input: "Build a TODO app"
    expected:
      tool_calls: [{ tool: Write, args: { path: "app.py" } }]

execution:            # Task (the agent under test)
  provider: claude
  model: claude-sonnet-4-5

evaluators:           # Scores
  - type: tool_trajectory
    match_mode: subset
```

## Open questions / variations
- **Where does config live?** Some frameworks (Braintrust) attach execution to task; AgentV makes it a top-level field.
- **Is expected output part of data or scores?** [[promptfoo]] inlines assertions into tests; AgentV separates them — both valid.
- **Multi-turn data shape**: a "datum" can be a single prompt, a conversation, or an environment seed. The triad survives but data schema explodes.

## AgentV implication
AgentV already follows the triad. The naming alignment with [[braintrust]] is intentional and a strategic moat for cross-framework imports. Any future schema change should preserve the triad — proposals that bundle task into evaluators or data into evaluators break portability.

- [[multi-judge-consensus]]
- [[tool-trajectory-evaluation]]
- [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]]

# Harness Quality Evaluation

## Definition
The strategic shift from evaluating **the model** ("did the agent pass?") to evaluating **how well the agent used its harness** ("did it use the right skill? the right MCP server? the right slash command?"). As harnesses gain skills, MCP servers, AGENTS.md files, and model configuration, harness usage becomes the dominant factor in agent outcomes — but most benchmarks still treat the agent as a black box.

## Origin / who uses it
- [[margin-evals]] — explicit framing of agent-vs-harness as separate eval axes.
- [[harness-engineered-pipelines]] — case studies of harness-driven outcome variance.
- [[agentv]]'s evaluator primitives (`tool_trajectory`, `llm_judge`, `composite`, `execution_metrics.exploration_ratio`) — already capable of scoring harness usage quality.
- [[everything-claude-code]] — measures Claude Code's behavior across harness configurations.
- From the research directory: `findings/harness-evaluation-differentiator/`.

## Why it matters
Holding the model constant, two harnesses can produce a 10-30 point swing on the same benchmark. The model is the same; the harness made the difference. Implications:
- **For users**: harness configuration is the highest-leverage knob, but no benchmark surfaces it.
- **For vendors**: a model that scores high on raw benchmarks may underperform a competitor with a better default harness.
- **For AgentV**: the framework that can score "harness usage quality" wins a moat that pure model benchmarks cannot reach.

This is AgentV's strategic differentiator. Treating the agent as a black box (the [[margin-evals]] approach) loses the signal entirely.

[[dev-workspaces]] is a useful boundary case here: it uses promptfoo to check whether a model can infer the intended workflow from a skill file and emit the expected JSON plan. That is valuable, but it still stops **before** harness quality evaluation begins. It validates the **predicted plan**, not the **actual runtime behavior**.

## What "harness usage quality" decomposes into
| Sub-metric | AgentV primitive | Question answered |
|---|---|---|
| Skill selection | `tool_trajectory` over skill-invocation events | Did it call the right skill? |
| MCP server choice | `tool_trajectory` with MCP-namespaced tools | Did it use the right MCP server? |
| Slash command usage | event-stream match against expected `/cmd` | Did it invoke the right shortcut? |
| Read-before-write | `execution_metrics.exploration_ratio` | Did it gather context first? |
| Token efficiency | tokens / pass | Did it solve the task economically? |
| Sub-agent dispatch | `TRANSFER` event matches | Did it delegate correctly? |
| Prompt/harness version linkage | span metadata + [[score-provenance]] | Which harness change caused the quality shift? |
| Live cost/latency behavior | cost and latency evaluators over traces | Did the harness pass without becoming too slow or expensive? |

The production LLM eval/observability loop adds an operational requirement: harness-quality scores need stable trace metadata. Without prompt version, harness version, model parameters, variant labels, and cost/latency fields, a passing or failing score cannot be tied back to the change that caused it.

## Implementation snapshot
```yaml
tests:
  - id: refactor-with-skill
    input: "Refactor auth.py to use the new error pattern"
    expected:
      tool_calls:
        - tool: skill.invoke
          args: { name: skill-creator }
        - tool: Read
          args: { path: "auth.py" }
        - tool: Write

evaluators:
  - type: tool_trajectory
    match_mode: subset
    weight: 0.5                  # did harness route correctly?
  - type: execution_metrics
    field: exploration_ratio
    min: 2.0                     # at least 2 reads per write
    weight: 0.2
  - type: llm_judge
    rubric: "Did the agent use the skill effectively?"
    weight: 0.3
```

## Open questions / variations
- **Is harness quality model-dependent?** A skill that helps Sonnet-4.5 may hurt Opus-4.7. Probably yes — needs per-model baselines.
- **Skill discoverability**: should the eval reward agents that *find* the right skill autonomously, or only those given it? Both — different evals.
- **Skill noise**: too many skills in the harness degrade selection. The "skill cliff" is real but unmeasured.
- **Comparability**: harness-A run on benchmark-X vs harness-B run on benchmark-X is interpretable; harness-A on benchmark-X vs harness-B on benchmark-Y is not.

## AgentV implication
AgentV already has the primitives. The remaining work is **packaging**:
1. A `harness_quality` evaluator preset that bundles the sub-metrics with sensible defaults.
2. Documentation framing AgentV as a "harness eval framework", not just a "model eval framework".
3. Default integration with [[paired-baseline-skill-eval]] so harness-with-X vs harness-without-X is the default mode.

This is the strategic moat. See [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] and `findings/harness-evaluation-differentiator/`.

# Evaluation Frameworks Landscape

Synthesis of 79 findings + 2 external sources covering the agent-evaluation space as of 2026. The goal: navigate quickly between every notable framework, benchmark, and pattern, and surface what's converged vs what's still divergent.

## What is converged (standardize now)
- **Data/Task/Scores triad** — universal. Every framework decomposes evaluation into these three pieces, even if it doesn't name them. See [[data-task-scores-triad]].
- **0–1 normalized score range** — universal. Most frameworks also expose a binary `pass` boolean derived from a threshold.
- **LLM-as-judge for subjective evaluation** — 19/21 surveyed.
- **Hybrid judging** (deterministic rule + LLM) — 15/17. [[kodus-ai]] productionizes this with parser-contract gates before LLM scoring.
- **JSON result schema** — `{ pass, score, reason, metric }` is the most-complete shape (promptfoo). nem035's `{ pass, reason, score? }` is emerging as a practical default.
- **CLI-first design** — 14/16 frameworks.
- **Verdict is binary, score is continuous, threshold is configurable** — no framework uses a named intermediate verdict between pass and fail (verdict-classification-industry-survey, 8 frameworks). [[agentv]] correctly removed `borderline`.

## What is emerging (monitor, may standardize next cycle)
- **Multi-judge consensus with variance penalty** — [[opencode-bench]] (3 judges, λ=0.5) + [[kodus-ai]] (dual Sonnet+GPT). See [[multi-judge-consensus]].
- **Agent-as-a-Judge for open-ended artifacts** — [[finresearchbench]] extracts a long financial research report into a logic tree before combining rule-derived metrics and LLM-scored dimensions. See [[agent-as-a-judge]].
- **Episode isolation & pass@k trials** — see [[episode-isolation]], [[pass-at-k]].
- **Variant systems** — Sniffbench's CLAUDE.md-hashing approach is unique today. See [[variant-systems]].
- **ACP protocol for CLI control** — all of Copilot, Claude, Gemini, Codex, Qwen support `--acp`. Standardization-ready. See [[agent-cli-control-pty-acp-sdk]].
- **OpenTelemetry for evals** — ADK-js, LangWatch, [[langfuse]], [[arize-phoenix]], [[vscode-copilot-agents]]. Subagent context propagation is the differentiator.
- **Red-teaming taxonomy** — promptfoo (50+ plugins, OWASP LLM Top 10) + DeepEval (40+ strategies) ≈ 90+ combined. See [[red-teaming-vulnerability-taxonomy]].
- **CI gating with metric hard gates** — wtg-agent-evaluator's `Gate(min_score=…)` independent of weighted score. See [[ci-gating-with-hard-gates]].
- **Cassette record/replay** for deterministic CI — [[runledger]]. See [[cassette-record-replay]].
- **Event-stream as eval source of truth** — JSONL traces enable online/offline parity (wtg-agent-evaluator, langfuse, langsmith). See [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]].

## What is too early (or framework-specific)
- Agent definition format — framework-specific by nature
- Prompt template format — keep flexible
- Execution engine internals — define interface, not engine
- Result storage backend — define output schema, not storage
- Multi-step evaluation pipelines (Mastra's 4-step is one shape; others are simpler)
- Scenario / user simulation — LangWatch only

## Framework matrix (28 surveyed)

### Eval frameworks & SDKs
| Framework | Lang | Type | Distinctive |
|---|---|---|---|
| [[agentv]] | TS | CLI + SDK | Reference impl of [[agentevals-open-standard]], coding-agent focus |
| [[deepeval]] | Py | pytest-style | 45+ metrics, G-Eval rubrics, 40+ red-team strategies |
| [[promptfoo]] | TS | YAML CLI | Matrix eval (prompts × providers × tests), 50+ assertions, red-teaming |
| [[braintrust]] | Py/TS | SaaS + SDK | Data/Task/Scores naming, "Loop AI" log query, trial variance |
| [[mastra]] | TS | SDK | Four-step `createScorer` pipeline, production sampling |
| [[langwatch]] | TS | SDK + platform | Strict `EvaluatorEntry` schema, dual eval/guardrail flag |
| [[langfuse]] | TS | OSS platform | ClickHouse scores, source-tagged Scores (ANNOTATION/API/EVAL), 10 observation types |
| [[inspect-ai]] | Py | Framework | UK AISI; Task/Solver/Scorer/Metric/ScoreReducer split |
| [[arize-phoenix]] | Py/TS | OSS platform | OTel-native, ClassificationEvaluator, score `direction` |
| [[trulens]] | Py | Framework | Selector pattern (`metric.on_input().on_output()`) |
| [[openai-evals]] | Py | Framework | YAML registry, Recorder/event abstraction, Solver pattern |
| [[lm-evaluation-harness]] | Py | Benchmark harness | 200+ tasks, Filter stage, Jinja2 task templates |
| [[ragas]] | Py | RAG-focused | Typed metric output (binary/discrete/continuous/ranking) |
| [[agentevals-langchain]] | Py+TS | Trajectory lib | 4 match modes, per-tool arg overrides, graph trajectories |
| [[agentevals-nem035]] | TS | Vitest-style CLI | Fluent assertions, pass@k, cost limits |
| [[kodus-ai]] | TS | Domain harness | Parser-contract gate + dual-judge + line-IoU |
| [[wtg-agent-evaluator]] | Py | Production CLI | Event-stream SOT, hard-gate CI, chat-to-tests |
| [[runledger]] | Py | CI harness | Cassette replay, language-agnostic stdio, baselines-as-code |
| [[margin-evals]] | Go | Runtime | Immutable run bundles, unified/direct agent config, resume |
| [[azure-ai-evaluation]] | Py | Enterprise SDK | 25+ evaluators, column mapping, multi-turn at base class |

### Benchmarks
| Benchmark | Tasks | Distinctive |
|---|---|---|
| [[swe-bench]] | 2000/500/300/300 | Three-layer Docker, FAIL_TO_PASS/PASS_TO_PASS |
| [[multi-swe-bench]] | 1632 across 7 langs | Multilingual SWE-Bench methodology |
| [[terminal-bench]] | ~100 | YAML tasks, canary GUID anti-contamination, tmux capture |
| [[terminal-bench-2]] | frontier | Dataset-only; Harbor runtime |
| [[opencode-bench]] | real commits | 3 judges × 5 weighted dims, variance penalty |
| [[sniffbench]] | comprehension/bootstrap/closed-issues | Variant system, exploration_ratio |
| [[mle-bench]] | 75 / 22 lite | Kaggle competitions, mean ± SEM seeds |
| [[nl2repo-bench]] | 104 | 0-to-1 repo generation from NL spec |
| [[convex-evals]] | 74 | Multi-step deploy+test pipeline, 35+ models leaderboard |
| [[skillsbench]] | 86 / 84 | First Skills-as-artifact benchmark; three-condition |
| [[finresearchbench]] | 70 | Financial deep-research reports scored by a logic-tree [[agent-as-a-judge]] |

### Orchestration & CLI control
| Tool | Distinctive |
|---|---|
| [[ralph-orchestrator]] | PTY-based universal control; backpressure-over-prescription eval philosophy |
| [[vibe-kanban]] | Raw ACP, git-worktree-per-task, session forking |
| [[copilot-sdk]] | Official GitHub SDK wrapping ACP; recommended over PTY/raw-ACP |
| pullfrog | GitHub Actions bot with Build/Review/Fix/Plan mode taxonomy |
| pentagi | Docker-isolated multi-agent pentest, Flow→Task→Subtask→Action |
| copilot-swarm-orchestrator | Transcript evidence-based drift detection |

### External sources (this wiki)
- [[dexter]] — minimal LangSmith eval runner reference with TUI
- [[vscode-copilot-agents]] — official OTel observability surface for Copilot agents

## Cluster map by methodology

**Coding-agent issue resolution** — [[swe-bench]], [[multi-swe-bench]], [[opencode-bench]]
**Terminal / system tasks** — [[terminal-bench]], [[terminal-bench-2]], [[skillsbench]] (all on Harbor)
**Greenfield code generation** — [[nl2repo-bench]], [[convex-evals]]
**ML-engineering long-horizon** — [[mle-bench]]
**A/B & variant** — [[sniffbench]], [[convex-evals]] (experiment tags), [[agentv]] (compare)

**Pytest/Vitest-style code-defined evals** — [[deepeval]], [[agentevals-nem035]]
**YAML-driven matrix/registry** — [[promptfoo]], [[openai-evals]], [[lm-evaluation-harness]], [[wtg-agent-evaluator]], [[agentv]]
**Composable scorer pipelines** — [[mastra]], [[braintrust]], [[trulens]]
**Trajectory-specialized** — [[agentevals-langchain]], [[wtg-agent-evaluator]]
**OSS observability + eval** — [[langfuse]], [[arize-phoenix]], [[langwatch]]
**Deterministic CI / regression** — [[runledger]], [[margin-evals]], [[wtg-agent-evaluator]]

## Recurring patterns
- [[data-task-scores-triad]]
- [[multi-judge-consensus]]
- [[agent-as-a-judge]]
- [[variant-systems]]
- [[tool-trajectory-evaluation]]

- [[karpathy-autoresearch-pattern]]
- [[harness-quality-evaluation]]
- [[paired-baseline-skill-eval]]
- [[scorer-metric-reducer]]

- [[run-bundles-and-baselines-as-code]]

## Cross-cluster themes
- **PTY is dying, JSON-RPC won**: [[ralph-orchestrator]] (PTY) → [[vibe-kanban]] (raw ACP) → [[copilot-sdk]] (typed SDK). Codex follows the same SDK→app-server pattern.
- **Harness quality is the new frontier**: pass/fail benchmarks measure the *naked agent*; the industry is moving toward evaluating whether agents *use their harness well* (skills, MCP, workspace conventions). See [[harness-quality-evaluation]].
- **Paired baseline is non-negotiable for skill evals**: skill-creator, Tessl, wtg-skills-autoresearch, microsoft-skills-harness all converge. Baseline isolation is an orchestration concern. See [[paired-baseline-skill-eval]].
- **Karpathy's autoresearch is the canonical optimization loop**: pi-autoresearch, wtg-skills-autoresearch, hermes-agent, agentic-harness-engineering all instantiate it. See [[karpathy-autoresearch-pattern]].
- **JSONL is the universal interchange format**: even Dolt-backed beads exports JSONL. Storage upgrades should layer on top.
- **Token efficiency dominates variance**: context-engineering-skills cites ~80% of performance variance attributable to token usage, ~10% tool calls, ~5% model. Behavior metrics like exploration_ratio (read/write) are differentiating.
