   140	    *.otlp.json                        Tempo / Honeycomb …
   141	```
   142	
   143	### Shared data model
   144	
   145	The only data model AgentV needs is the **OTel span tree with GenAI semantic-convention attributes**:
   146	
   147	- Root: `invoke_agent` (rename from current `agentv.eval` for non-eval flows; keep both)
   148	- Children: `chat` (LLM calls), `execute_tool` (tool invocations), `agentv.turn.N` (turn grouping)
   149	- Attributes: `gen_ai.operation.name`, `gen_ai.provider.name` (replacing deprecated `gen_ai.system`), `gen_ai.request.model`, `gen_ai.usage.{input,output}_tokens`, `gen_ai.tool.name`, `gen_ai.tool.call.{id,arguments,result}`
   150	- Resource attributes: `service.name` (= `agentv` or the agent provider name, mirroring VS Code Copilot's `service.name` taxonomy), `service.version`
   151	
   152	**Score attachment:** scores become OTel **span events** (this is what the code does already) with attributes `agentv.score.{name,value,verdict,source}`. This maps cleanly onto:
   153	
   154	- Phoenix's `Annotation { kind: "LLM" | "CODE" | "HUMAN" }`
   155	- Langfuse's `Score.source = "ANNOTATION" | "API" | "EVAL"`
   156	- LangWatch's `is_guardrail` dual-use flag
   157	
   158	Adopting score `source` provenance (see [[score-provenance]]) closes [[arize-phoenix]] and [[langfuse]] convergence gaps the wiki has already flagged.
   159	
   160	### Why offline and online share one model
   161	
   162	The trick to making offline-eval and live-obs the same product is **the span IS the result**.
   163	
   164	- An offline eval produces a JSONL + an OTLP-JSON file. Same spans, written to disk.
   165	- A live observability session produces an OTLP/HTTP stream to Phoenix/Langfuse/Aspire. Same spans, written to network.
   166	- Re-scoring a historical run is reading the OTLP-JSON file back and running graders against it. No re-invocation, no re-cost.
   167	
   168	This is exactly the [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]] pattern, just spelled in OTel instead of WTG's JSONL: every metric computed from one canonical event stream, online and offline aggregators share code. AgentV's existing `computeTraceSummary(output)` is the offline aggregator; the streaming observer is the online one. **They should consume the same span representation.** Today they don't quite — `computeTraceSummary` builds a `TraceSummary` from messages, while the streaming observer emits OTel spans. Unifying them on a shared OTel-shaped in-memory model is the cleanup.
   169	
   170	---
   171	
   172	## 4. MVP — what to implement first
   173	
   174	Three releasable slices, smallest blast radius first. Each one closes an existing tracked issue.
   175	
   176	### MVP-1 (1 week): finish GenAI semconv alignment and ship the Aspire docs recipe
   177	
   178	Closes #298, finishes #299, validates #301 + #302.
   179	
   180	- Replace `gen_ai.system = 'agentv'` with `gen_ai.provider.name = '<provider>'` (or keep both for one release; deprecate the old form). Spec is in Development but the rename is settled.
   181	- Add `service.name = '<provider-name>'` and `gen_ai.agent.name = '<target.name>'` as resource attributes. Match the VS Code Copilot taxonomy (`copilot-chat`, `github-copilot`, `claude-code`, …) so cross-tool traces are queryable together.
   182	- Add `REDACTED` honoring once the spec settles further.
   183	- Docs: a 6-line `docker run mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspire-dashboard:latest` recipe at `apps/web/src/content/docs/observability/local-viewer.md`. Tested screenshot. Done.
   184	
   185	Acceptance signal: a user can run `agentv run my.eval.yaml --export-otel` and see the trace in Aspire Dashboard in under 5 minutes.
   186	
   187	### MVP-2 (1–2 weeks): `agentv watch` — observability outside of evals
   188	
   189	The big architectural change. Closes #300.
   190	
   191	- New command: `agentv watch <agent-target>` — wires the same `OtelStreamingObserver` around a free-form agent invocation, no test case required, no graders. Inputs from stdin or `--input` file; output streamed to the configured OTLP endpoint.
   192	- Subagent propagation: set `TRACEPARENT` + `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` in the child process env so any agentv-instrumented subprocess (or anything reading W3C tracecontext, like Copilot CLI) becomes a child span automatically.
   193	- Optionally: a `--collect` flag that writes traces to `.agentv/traces/watch-<ts>.otlp.json` for later replay.
   194	
   195	Acceptance signal: `agentv watch claude-sdk -i prompt.md` produces a complete OTel trace tree visible in any OTLP backend. A subsequent `agentv eval <file> --replay .agentv/traces/watch-<ts>.otlp.json` re-scores it without re-running the agent.
   196	
   197	### MVP-3 (2–3 weeks): in-flight scoring
   198	
   199	The "real-time eval" piece. Closes #305 properly.
   200	
   201	Two complementary paths — ship whichever fits the user's posture:
   202	
   203	- **Tap-and-score (in-process)**: in `agentv watch`, run lightweight graders as spans close. Latency and cost graders trigger on `chat` span end. Tool-call assertion graders trigger on `execute_tool` end. Guardrail-style LLM graders run async on a sampled subset (mirrors [[langfuse]]'s `JobConfiguration { delay, samplingPct }`). Emit score results as span events on the parent — they show up in any backend automatically.
   204	- **Forward-and-defer**: emit only; rely on Phoenix online evaluators or Langfuse jobs to score the stream. AgentV becomes the emitter; the backend is the scorer. This is cheaper for users who already run Phoenix.
   205	
   206	The default should be Tap-and-score with the lightweight deterministic graders (latency, cost, token_usage, regex, contains, execution_metrics) and `--score-async` for LLM-based ones. This is consistent with [[langwatch]]'s `is_guardrail` dual-use boolean — every grader becomes either online (guardrail) or offline (analytical) based on a single flag.
   207	
   208	Acceptance signal: an `agentv watch` session with `--score latency,cost,tool_trajectory` emits scores into the live trace within ~100 ms of each span closing, and they render as Phoenix `Annotation`s / Langfuse `Score`s.
   209	
   210	### What to *not* build for MVP
   211	
   212	- A hosted dashboard. Aspire / Phoenix / Langfuse already exist. The product-strategy in [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] is explicit about this.
   213	- A new wire format. OTel + OTLP is the format.
   214	- A built-in collector. The OTel Collector exists; document it.
   215	- Auth, multi-tenancy, retention policies. That's what Phoenix/Langfuse/Datadog do.
   216	
   217	---
   218	
   219	## 5. Offline evals and live observability — how they coexist
   220	
   221	The way to frame it for the docs:
   222	
   223	> **AgentV is an agent telemetry source.** Offline evals are telemetry generated under a structured test plan with graders attached. Live observability is the same telemetry generated under whatever the user happens to be doing. The data model is identical. The difference is the input scaffolding and which graders fire when.
   224	
   225	Concretely:
   226	
   227	| | Offline eval (`agentv run`) | Live observability (`agentv watch`) |
   228	|---|---|---|
   229	| Input | YAML test cases | stdin / prompt file / piped CLI |
   230	| Iteration | One per test case, repeated for trials | One per invocation; no loop |

     3	created: 2026-06-06
     4	updated: 2026-06-06
     5	type: concept
     6	tags: [evaluation, benchmark, framework, harness-engineering, synthesis]

     8	---
     9	
    10	# Benchmark Provenance and Workspace Patterns
    11	
    12	Eval frameworks need to separate **source provenance**, **operational setup**, and **scoring artifacts**. When those are collapsed into one schema, the framework accumulates source-specific fields. When they are separated, [[agentv]] can stay a small execution layer while still representing benchmark-style suites through metadata, hooks, case directories, graders, and result bundles.
    13	
    14	## Durable pattern
    15	
    16	| Concern | Durable representation | Avoid |
    17	|---|---|---|
    18	| Source provenance | Frozen source pins in `metadata`, source task packs, generator manifests, case IDs, row IDs, source commits | A top-level schema field for every benchmark family |
    19	| Operational setup | Workspace repos, lifecycle hooks, Dockerfile/image/Compose templates, setup scripts that receive case metadata | Mixing informational provenance with setup fields |
    20	| Gold data and oracles | Expected output, hidden tests, code graders, shell tests, case-directory fixtures | Treating oracle validation as a special runtime mode |
    21	| Score provenance | Score source/kind, evaluator ID, explanation/evidence, raw assertion arrays | Anonymous scores with no judge/metric/human distinction |
    22	| Run artifacts | Immutable result directories with logs, traces, file diffs, grader output, and workspace references | Dashboard-only state or mutable run summaries |
    23	
    24	The practical rule for AgentV docs is: use metadata for audit context, hooks for setup, graders for verification, and generated files/templates for ceremony reduction. Add schema only for universal meaning that would otherwise be lost, such as rubric criterion operators in [[agentv-eval-authoring-extensibility]].
    25	
    26	## Peer-framework lessons
    27	
    28	- [[promptfoo]] maps well to prompt/provider/test matrices and JavaScript assertions. It can approximate workflow-plan or rubric checks, but workspace setup, live tool trajectories, and full AgentV code-grader payloads require custom adapters.
    29	- [[braintrust]] maps cleanly to Data/Task/Scores and trial-count loops. Rich AgentV assertions, trace summaries, cost, duration, and file-change evidence usually become score metadata unless a deeper adapter exists.
    30	- [[arize-phoenix]] is strongest for persisted datasets, experiments, score annotations, and OpenTelemetry traces. Its normal experiment flow assumes a Phoenix dataset/server round trip, so local YAML-style benchmark authoring needs wrapper scripts rather than core AgentV schema changes.
    31	- [[margin-evals]], [[terminal-bench]], [[terminal-bench-2]], and [[skillsbench]] show the benchmark-runtime side: filesystem-native cases, fresh isolated environments, post-agent test staging, hidden/reference solution separation, and structured artifacts.
    32	
    33	## Container/workspace pattern
    34	
    35	Terminal-style benchmarks converge on fresh execution identities per task or trial, with reusable environment assets:
    36	
    37	- Use a fresh container, workspace, or Compose project identity per case/trial.
    38	- Allow Dockerfile builds, prebuilt images, and Compose overlays where multi-service dependencies matter.
    39	- Cache or digest-pin images so reproducibility and runtime cost are visible.
    40	- Stage hidden tests after agent execution when the benchmark needs to prevent direct inspection.
    41	- Persist logs, test output, traces, artifacts, and run manifests in a structured bundle.
    42	
    43	For AgentV, these are principles to document and template, not a mandate to import Harbor's full task schema or Margin's single-container assumptions. A minimal AgentV workspace/container primitive should remain small enough that plain lifecycle hooks and case directories still cover most benchmark authors.
    44	
    45	## Public-safe boundary
    46	
    47	Private parity work is useful because it exposes ceremony and lossy mappings against real corpora. The public wiki and AgentV docs should keep only the generalizable findings:
    48	
    49	- which primitives map cleanly across frameworks
    50	- which mappings require adapters or templates
    51	- which AgentV gaps lose source meaning
    52	- which peer features are useful patterns but not core schema
    53	
    54	Do not copy private competitor artifacts, confidential task details, or organization-specific setup into public examples. Promote sanitized patterns only after hand ports prove they recur.
    55	
    56	## Cross-refs
    57	
    58	- [[agentv]]
    59	- [[agentv-eval-authoring-extensibility]]
    60	- [[promptfoo-vs-braintrust-vs-agentv]]
    61	- [[agentv-vs-margin-evals]]
    62	- [[score-provenance]]
    63	- [[tool-trajectory-evaluation]]

     2	title: Production LLM Evaluation and Observability Loop
     3	created: 2026-06-04
     4	updated: 2026-06-04
     5	type: concept
     6	tags: [evaluation, production-ai, framework, serving, synthesis]
     7	sources: [raw/articles/llm-evaluation-observability-best-practices.md, raw/articles/claude-code-agent-farm-best-practices-guides.md]
     8	---
     9	
    10	# Production LLM Evaluation and Observability Loop
    11	
    12	## Definition
    13	
    14	A production operating loop for LLM applications where prompts, model calls, traces, eval datasets, scores, dashboards, rollouts, and human feedback are linked by stable IDs. The core idea is that evaluation is not a separate offline ritual: the same instrumentation that powers live observability should also power regression tests, CI gates, cost controls, and post-deployment quality analysis.
    15	
    16	The pattern generalizes the stack in `LLM_EVALUATION_AND_OBSERVABILITY_BEST_PRACTICES.md`: Langfuse or a similar backend for production traces, [[arize-phoenix]] for local trace debugging, [[ragas]] or custom metrics for semantic scoring, and CI/rollout gates to prevent bad prompt or model changes from reaching all users.
    17	
    18	## Operating loop
    19	
    20	1. **Version every prompt and harness input.** Prompt templates, model parameters, tool lists, retrieval configuration, safety settings, and evaluator thresholds are deployable artifacts.
    21	2. **Instrument once at the model/client boundary.** Every generation records prompt version, model, parameters, token usage, latency, cost, cache status, trace ID, user/session metadata, and variant/experiment labels.
    22	3. **Run fast gates before expensive judges.** Schema checks, JSON parsing, safety regexes, length bounds, and contract tests fail quickly before LLM judges or human review spend more tokens.
    23	4. **Score semantically where deterministic checks are insufficient.** RAG faithfulness, answer relevance, structured extraction accuracy, tool-trajectory quality, and safety/compliance belong in separate score fields with provenance.
    24	5. **Gate CI and rollout separately.** CI hard gates block known regressions; progressive rollout watches live metrics under real traffic and trips circuit breakers on latency, cost, error, or quality regressions.
    25	6. **Feed production traces back into evals.** Failed traces, high-cost traces, human-corrected outputs, and near-miss rollout incidents become new eval cases.
    26	
    27	## Why it matters
    28	
    29	This loop closes a gap between offline eval frameworks and production observability platforms. Offline evals are useful but stale if they do not ingest production failures. Observability is useful but passive if traces do not become tests and gates. The durable system ties them together: every prompt/model/harness change gets evaluated before merge, watched during rollout, and mined after deployment.
    30	
    31	The same principle applies to coding agents. A future [[agentv]] live-observability surface should not just show traces; it should collect traces that can be replayed, rescored, and turned into [[harness-quality-evaluation]] cases. That makes [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]] the shared substrate for both offline evaluation and production monitoring.
    32	
    33	## Evaluation layers
    34	
    35	| Layer | Examples | Role |
    36	|---|---|---|
    37	| Contract checks | JSON/schema validity, required fields, tool args | Fail malformed outputs cheaply |
    38	| Fast filters | unsafe substrings, PII patterns, length and latency bounds | Block obvious failures before semantic scoring |
    39	| Domain metrics | [[ragas]] faithfulness/relevance, extraction accuracy, task-specific rubrics | Measure task quality |
    40	| Harness metrics | tool trajectory, exploration ratio, token/pass, delegation events | Measure how the agent used its environment |
    41	| Human review | critical workflows, calibration sets, disputed judge calls | Correct and calibrate automated scores |
    42	| Rollout monitors | success rate, P95 latency, cost/session, cache hit rate, error rate | Catch production regressions |
    43	
    44	## AgentV implication
    45	
    46	For [[agentv]], this pattern argues for one shared trace model across `agentv run` and any future `agentv watch` surface:
    47	
    48	- Offline eval runs emit the same spans that live observability emits.
    49	- Scores attach to spans with [[score-provenance]] so dashboards can separate code judges, LLM judges, human annotations, and deterministic metrics.
    50	- Cost, latency, cache hit rate, and token usage are first-class evaluators, not only dashboard fields.
    51	- CI gates and rollout gates are different consumers of the same scores: CI blocks merge, rollout blocks traffic expansion.
    52	- Production traces can be replayed into eval suites without re-running the model when the captured event stream is complete enough.
    53	
    54	## Open questions
    55	
    56	- How much production trace data should be sampled into evals by default?
    57	- Which traces need human review before becoming golden cases?
    58	- Should cost and latency gates be absolute thresholds, regression thresholds, or both?
    59	- How should semantic-cache hits be evaluated when they avoid a model call but may preserve stale behavior?
    60	
    61	## Cross-refs
    62	
    63	- [[agentv-realtime-observability]]
    64	- [[ci-gating-with-hard-gates]]
    65	- [[harness-quality-evaluation]]
    66	- [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]]
    67	- [[score-provenance]]
    68	- [[evaluation-frameworks-landscape]]
    69	- [[arize-phoenix]]
    70	- [[langfuse]]
    71	- [[ragas]]
    72	
    73	## Sources
    74	
    75	- `raw/articles/llm-evaluation-observability-best-practices.md`
    76	- `raw/articles/claude-code-agent-farm-best-practices-guides.md`

     1	---
     2	title: Harness Quality Evaluation
     3	created: 2026-05-12
     4	updated: 2026-06-04
     5	type: concept
     6	tags: [evaluation, agentic, framework, prediction, synthesis]
     7	sources: [raw/articles/dexter-evals.md, raw/articles/vscode-copilot-monitoring-agents.md, raw/articles/dev-workspaces-evals.md, raw/articles/openai-harness-engineering.md, raw/articles/llm-evaluation-observability-best-practices.md]
     8	---
     9	
    10	# Harness Quality Evaluation
    11	
    12	## Definition
    13	The strategic shift from evaluating **the model** ("did the agent pass?") to evaluating **how well the agent used its harness** ("did it use the right skill? the right MCP server? the right slash command?"). As harnesses gain skills, MCP servers, AGENTS.md files, and model configuration, harness usage becomes the dominant factor in agent outcomes — but most benchmarks still treat the agent as a black box.
    14	
    15	## Origin / who uses it
    16	- [[margin-evals]] — explicit framing of agent-vs-harness as separate eval axes.
    17	- [[harness-engineered-pipelines]] — case studies of harness-driven outcome variance.
    18	- [[agentv]]'s evaluator primitives (`tool_trajectory`, `llm_judge`, `composite`, `execution_metrics.exploration_ratio`) — already capable of scoring harness usage quality.
    19	- [[everything-claude-code]] — measures Claude Code's behavior across harness configurations.
    20	- From the research directory: `findings/harness-evaluation-differentiator/`.
    21	
    22	## Why it matters
    23	Holding the model constant, two harnesses can produce a 10-30 point swing on the same benchmark. The model is the same; the harness made the difference. Implications:
    24	- **For users**: harness configuration is the highest-leverage knob, but no benchmark surfaces it.
    25	- **For vendors**: a model that scores high on raw benchmarks may underperform a competitor with a better default harness.
    26	- **For AgentV**: the framework that can score "harness usage quality" wins a moat that pure model benchmarks cannot reach.
    27	
    28	This is AgentV's strategic differentiator. Treating the agent as a black box (the [[margin-evals]] approach) loses the signal entirely.
    29	
    30	[[dev-workspaces]] is a useful boundary case here: it uses promptfoo to check whether a model can infer the intended workflow from a skill file and emit the expected JSON plan. That is valuable, but it still stops **before** harness quality evaluation begins. It validates the **predicted plan**, not the **actual runtime behavior**.
    31	
    32	## What "harness usage quality" decomposes into
    33	| Sub-metric | AgentV primitive | Question answered |
    34	|---|---|---|
    35	| Skill selection | `tool_trajectory` over skill-invocation events | Did it call the right skill? |
    36	| MCP server choice | `tool_trajectory` with MCP-namespaced tools | Did it use the right MCP server? |
    37	| Slash command usage | event-stream match against expected `/cmd` | Did it invoke the right shortcut? |
    38	| Read-before-write | `execution_metrics.exploration_ratio` | Did it gather context first? |
    39	| Token efficiency | tokens / pass | Did it solve the task economically? |
    40	| Sub-agent dispatch | `TRANSFER` event matches | Did it delegate correctly? |
    41	| Prompt/harness version linkage | span metadata + [[score-provenance]] | Which harness change caused the quality shift? |
    42	| Live cost/latency behavior | cost and latency evaluators over traces | Did the harness pass without becoming too slow or expensive? |
    43	
    44	The production LLM eval/observability loop adds an operational requirement: harness-quality scores need stable trace metadata. Without prompt version, harness version, model parameters, variant labels, and cost/latency fields, a passing or failing score cannot be tied back to the change that caused it.
    45	
    46	## Implementation snapshot
    47	```yaml
    48	tests:
    49	  - id: refactor-with-skill
    50	    input: "Refactor auth.py to use the new error pattern"
    51	    expected:
    52	      tool_calls:
    53	        - tool: skill.invoke
    54	          args: { name: skill-creator }
    55	        - tool: Read
    56	          args: { path: "auth.py" }
    57	        - tool: Write
    58	
    59	evaluators:
    60	  - type: tool_trajectory
    61	    match_mode: subset
    62	    weight: 0.5                  # did harness route correctly?
    63	  - type: execution_metrics
    64	    field: exploration_ratio
    65	    min: 2.0                     # at least 2 reads per write
    66	    weight: 0.2
    67	  - type: llm_judge
    68	    rubric: "Did the agent use the skill effectively?"
    69	    weight: 0.3
    70	```
    71	
    72	## Open questions / variations
    73	- **Is harness quality model-dependent?** A skill that helps Sonnet-4.5 may hurt Opus-4.7. Probably yes — needs per-model baselines.
    74	- **Skill discoverability**: should the eval reward agents that *find* the right skill autonomously, or only those given it? Both — different evals.
    75	- **Skill noise**: too many skills in the harness degrade selection. The "skill cliff" is real but unmeasured.
    76	- **Comparability**: harness-A run on benchmark-X vs harness-B run on benchmark-X is interpretable; harness-A on benchmark-X vs harness-B on benchmark-Y is not.
    77	
    78	## AgentV implication
    79	AgentV already has the primitives. The remaining work is **packaging**:
    80	1. A `harness_quality` evaluator preset that bundles the sub-metrics with sensible defaults.
    81	2. Documentation framing AgentV as a "harness eval framework", not just a "model eval framework".
    82	3. Default integration with [[paired-baseline-skill-eval]] so harness-with-X vs harness-without-X is the default mode.
    83	
    84	This is the strategic moat. See [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] and `findings/harness-evaluation-differentiator/`.
    85

     9	
    10	# Evaluation Frameworks Landscape
    11	
    12	Synthesis of 79 findings + 2 external sources covering the agent-evaluation space as of 2026. The goal: navigate quickly between every notable framework, benchmark, and pattern, and surface what's converged vs what's still divergent.
    13	
    14	## What is converged (standardize now)
    15	- **Data/Task/Scores triad** — universal. Every framework decomposes evaluation into these three pieces, even if it doesn't name them. See [[data-task-scores-triad]].
    16	- **0–1 normalized score range** — universal. Most frameworks also expose a binary `pass` boolean derived from a threshold.
    17	- **LLM-as-judge for subjective evaluation** — 19/21 surveyed.
    18	- **Hybrid judging** (deterministic rule + LLM) — 15/17. [[kodus-ai]] productionizes this with parser-contract gates before LLM scoring.
    19	- **JSON result schema** — `{ pass, score, reason, metric }` is the most-complete shape (promptfoo). nem035's `{ pass, reason, score? }` is emerging as a practical default.
    20	- **CLI-first design** — 14/16 frameworks.
    21	- **Verdict is binary, score is continuous, threshold is configurable** — no framework uses a named intermediate verdict between pass and fail (verdict-classification-industry-survey, 8 frameworks). [[agentv]] correctly removed `borderline`.
    22	
    23	## What is emerging (monitor, may standardize next cycle)
    24	- **Multi-judge consensus with variance penalty** — [[opencode-bench]] (3 judges, λ=0.5) + [[kodus-ai]] (dual Sonnet+GPT). See [[multi-judge-consensus]].
    25	- **Agent-as-a-Judge for open-ended artifacts** — [[finresearchbench]] extracts a long financial research report into a logic tree before combining rule-derived metrics and LLM-scored dimensions. See [[agent-as-a-judge]].
    26	- **Episode isolation & pass@k trials** — see [[episode-isolation]], [[pass-at-k]].
    27	- **Variant systems** — Sniffbench's CLAUDE.md-hashing approach is unique today. See [[variant-systems]].
    28	- **ACP protocol for CLI control** — all of Copilot, Claude, Gemini, Codex, Qwen support `--acp`. Standardization-ready. See [[agent-cli-control-pty-acp-sdk]].
    29	- **OpenTelemetry for evals** — ADK-js, LangWatch, [[langfuse]], [[arize-phoenix]], [[vscode-copilot-agents]]. Subagent context propagation is the differentiator.
    30	- **Red-teaming taxonomy** — promptfoo (50+ plugins, OWASP LLM Top 10) + DeepEval (40+ strategies) ≈ 90+ combined. See [[red-teaming-vulnerability-taxonomy]].
    31	- **CI gating with metric hard gates** — wtg-agent-evaluator's `Gate(min_score=…)` independent of weighted score. See [[ci-gating-with-hard-gates]].
    32	- **Cassette record/replay** for deterministic CI — [[runledger]]. See [[cassette-record-replay]].
    33	- **Event-stream as eval source of truth** — JSONL traces enable online/offline parity (wtg-agent-evaluator, langfuse, langsmith). See [[event-stream-as-source-of-truth]].
    34	
    35	## What is too early (or framework-specific)
    36	- Agent definition format — framework-specific by nature
    37	- Prompt template format — keep flexible
    38	- Execution engine internals — define interface, not engine
    39	- Result storage backend — define output schema, not storage
    40	- Multi-step evaluation pipelines (Mastra's 4-step is one shape; others are simpler)
    41	- Scenario / user simulation — LangWatch only
    42	
    43	## Framework matrix (28 surveyed)
    44	
    45	### Eval frameworks & SDKs
    46	| Framework | Lang | Type | Distinctive |
    47	|---|---|---|---|
    48	| [[agentv]] | TS | CLI + SDK | Reference impl of [[agentevals-open-standard]], coding-agent focus |
    49	| [[deepeval]] | Py | pytest-style | 45+ metrics, G-Eval rubrics, 40+ red-team strategies |
    50	| [[promptfoo]] | TS | YAML CLI | Matrix eval (prompts × providers × tests), 50+ assertions, red-teaming |
    51	| [[braintrust]] | Py/TS | SaaS + SDK | Data/Task/Scores naming, "Loop AI" log query, trial variance |
    52	| [[mastra]] | TS | SDK | Four-step `createScorer` pipeline, production sampling |
    53	| [[langwatch]] | TS | SDK + platform | Strict `EvaluatorEntry` schema, dual eval/guardrail flag |
    54	| [[langfuse]] | TS | OSS platform | ClickHouse scores, source-tagged Scores (ANNOTATION/API/EVAL), 10 observation types |
    55	| [[inspect-ai]] | Py | Framework | UK AISI; Task/Solver/Scorer/Metric/ScoreReducer split |
    56	| [[arize-phoenix]] | Py/TS | OSS platform | OTel-native, ClassificationEvaluator, score `direction` |
    57	| [[trulens]] | Py | Framework | Selector pattern (`metric.on_input().on_output()`) |
    58	| [[openai-evals]] | Py | Framework | YAML registry, Recorder/event abstraction, Solver pattern |
    59	| [[lm-evaluation-harness]] | Py | Benchmark harness | 200+ tasks, Filter stage, Jinja2 task templates |
    60	| [[ragas]] | Py | RAG-focused | Typed metric output (binary/discrete/continuous/ranking) |
    61	| [[agentevals-langchain]] | Py+TS | Trajectory lib | 4 match modes, per-tool arg overrides, graph trajectories |
    62	| [[agentevals-nem035]] | TS | Vitest-style CLI | Fluent assertions, pass@k, cost limits |
    63	| [[kodus-ai]] | TS | Domain harness | Parser-contract gate + dual-judge + line-IoU |
    64	| [[wtg-agent-evaluator]] | Py | Production CLI | Event-stream SOT, hard-gate CI, chat-to-tests |
    65	| [[runledger]] | Py | CI harness | Cassette replay, language-agnostic stdio, baselines-as-code |
    66	| [[margin-evals]] | Go | Runtime | Immutable run bundles, unified/direct agent config, resume |
    67	| [[azure-ai-evaluation]] | Py | Enterprise SDK | 25+ evaluators, column mapping, multi-turn at base class |
    68	
    69	### Benchmarks
    70	| Benchmark | Tasks | Distinctive |
    71	|---|---|---|
    72	| [[swe-bench]] | 2000/500/300/300 | Three-layer Docker, FAIL_TO_PASS/PASS_TO_PASS |
    73	| [[multi-swe-bench]] | 1632 across 7 langs | Multilingual SWE-Bench methodology |
    74	| [[terminal-bench]] | ~100 | YAML tasks, canary GUID anti-contamination, tmux capture |
    75	| [[terminal-bench-2]] | frontier | Dataset-only; Harbor runtime |
    76	| [[opencode-bench]] | real commits | 3 judges × 5 weighted dims, variance penalty |
    77	| [[sniffbench]] | comprehension/bootstrap/closed-issues | Variant system, exploration_ratio |
    78	| [[mle-bench]] | 75 / 22 lite | Kaggle competitions, mean ± SEM seeds |
    79	| [[nl2repo-bench]] | 104 | 0-to-1 repo generation from NL spec |
    80	| [[convex-evals]] | 74 | Multi-step deploy+test pipeline, 35+ models leaderboard |
    81	| [[skillsbench]] | 86 / 84 | First Skills-as-artifact benchmark; three-condition |
    82	| [[finresearchbench]] | 70 | Financial deep-research reports scored by a logic-tree [[agent-as-a-judge]] |
    83	
    84	### Orchestration & CLI control
    85	| Tool | Distinctive |
    86	|---|---|
    87	| [[ralph-orchestrator]] | PTY-based universal control; backpressure-over-prescription eval philosophy |
    88	| [[vibe-kanban]] | Raw ACP, git-worktree-per-task, session forking |
    89	| [[copilot-sdk]] | Official GitHub SDK wrapping ACP; recommended over PTY/raw-ACP |
    90	| pullfrog | GitHub Actions bot with Build/Review/Fix/Plan mode taxonomy |
    91	| pentagi | Docker-isolated multi-agent pentest, Flow→Task→Subtask→Action |
    92	| copilot-swarm-orchestrator | Transcript evidence-based drift detection |
    93

payload.message:
Yes, `agentv prepare` should probably generate the task prompt, but it should **not send it to the agent by default**.

If AgentV sends the prompt and launches the agent, that is no longer “prepare”; it is an eval run or a watched run. `prepare` should stop at a reproducible handoff package:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --out /tmp/agentv-case-1
```

It should produce:

```text
/tmp/agentv-case-1/
  workspace/              # materialized repo/template/hooks applied
  prompt.md               # task prompt to give the human/agent
  agentv_prepare.json     # eval path, test_id, target, hook order, repo pins, created_at
```

The prompt should include the task input and workspace instructions, but not expected output, hidden tests, grader details, or rubric internals unless explicitly allowed.

**Use Case**
The real use case is not “human evals.” It is better named:

- prepared attempt
- manual harness run
- external-agent attempt
- human-operated agent session

The problem being solved:

1. Reproduce one eval case in the exact AgentV workspace so a human can debug or dogfood it.
2. Let a human operate Codex/Claude/Copilot manually in that workspace.
3. Import the resulting session/transcript afterward.
4. Run AgentV graders on the final workspace state plus optional trajectory.

That suggests this command pair:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --out /tmp/case-1

# human runs agent manually in /tmp/case-1/workspace

agentv grade evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 \
  --prepared /tmp/case-1 \
  --transcript ~/.codex/sessions/session.jsonl
```

If transcript import needs normalization, make that internal at first. Later, expose it if needed:

```bash
agentv session import --provider codex --from ~/.codex/sessions/session.jsonl --out trace.agentv.jsonl
```

**Why This Exists**
The research wiki supports the underlying problem, but it argues for separating concerns. Workspace setup, scoring artifacts, and provenance should stay separate: [benchmark-provenance-workspace-patterns.md](/home/entity/projects/tsoyang-org-wiki/ai-research-wiki/concepts/benchmark-provenance-workspace-patterns.md:12). It also says live traces should become replayable/rescorable eval material, not just dashboard output: [production-llm-evaluation-observability-loop.md](/home/entity/projects/tsoyang-org-wiki/ai-research-wiki/concepts/production-llm-evaluation-observability-loop.md:25).

Peer frameworks show the same need exists:

- OpenAI sandbox docs explicitly call out workflows needing directories, files, commands, artifacts, exposed ports, and pausing for human review in the same workspace: [OpenAI Sandbox Agents](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes).
- SWE-bench separates environment setup, patch application, tests, and resolution judgment in its harness: [SWE-bench harness](https://www.swebench.com/SWE-bench/reference/harness/).
- Inspect has sandbox environments for agent tools to execute code in richer environments: [Inspect sandboxing](https://inspect.aisi.org.uk/sandboxing.html).
- Braintrust, LangSmith, Langfuse, and Phoenix all show the trace/dataset feedback loop: [Braintrust eval cycle](https://www.braintrust.dev/docs/evaluate), [LangSmith traces to dataset](https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/manage-datasets), [Langfuse datasets from traces](https://langfuse.com/docs/evaluation/experiments/datasets), [Phoenix datasets](https://arize.com/docs/phoenix/datasets-and-experiments/overview-datasets).

So I’d reframe #1263 away from `agentv watch`. The stronger primitive is:

```text
prepare workspace -> human/external agent works -> import session -> grade prepared attempt
```

That is a real AgentV-shaped workflow. `watch` is only justified later if AgentV itself needs to launch and observe arbitrary non-eval agent sessions live.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text: i don't think we need watch, can just remove it. just need prepare workspace. that achieves what the orginal issue was asking for anyway. if we want otel we can configure hookks in the harness itself (e.g. opik) and agentv can grade the opik traces

payload.message: i don't think we need watch, can just remove it. just need prepare workspace. that achieves what the orginal issue was asking for anyway. if we want otel we can configure hookks in the harness itself (e.g. opik) and agentv can grade the opik traces

I agree. Drop `agentv watch`.

The cleaner product boundary is:

```text
agentv prepare -> human/external agent works -> agentv grade/import traces
```

`prepare` solves the useful part of the original idea: AgentV materializes the exact eval workspace and prompt without claiming an eval has run.

For observability, AgentV does not need a live non-eval command. The harness or target can be configured to emit traces directly to Opik/Phoenix/OTel through env/config/hooks. Then AgentV can later grade either:

```bash
agentv grade evals/foo.eval.yaml --prepared /tmp/case-1
```

or, when trajectory matters:

```bash
agentv grade evals/foo.eval.yaml --prepared /tmp/case-1 \
  --trace opik:<project>/<trace-id>
```

Internally that should route through the normalized trajectory importer work, not a `watch` surface.

So the revised stance is:

- Close or supersede `agentv watch`.
- Create/reframe work as `agentv prepare`.
- Keep Opik/OTel as export/import integrations, not as a new AgentV live runner.
- Grade Opik traces only after mapping them into AgentV’s normalized trace/trajectory model.

That fits the AgentV guidelines better: workspace setup is a core primitive; live observability product surface belongs in Opik/Phoenix/harness hooks.

- Grade Opik traces only after mapping them into AgentV’s normalized trace/trajectory model.

That fits the AgentV guidelines better: workspace setup is a core primitive; live observability product surface belongs in Opik/Phoenix/harness hooks.

{"body":"## Context

[#298–#306](https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv/issues?q=is%3Aissue+author%3A%40me+sort%3Acreated-desc+298..306) shipped the OTel foundation:

- `OtelTraceExporter` + `OtlpJsonFileExporter` + Langfuse/Braintrust/Confident presets
- `OtelStreamingObserver` emits real-time `agentv.eval` / `chat` / `execute_tool` spans during eval execution
- W3C `traceparent` propagation, GenAI per-span tokens, turn-level span grouping

**The remaining gap:** all of this is reachable only through the eval orchestrator. A user who just wants to *watch* an agent run (no test case, no graders) has no surface today. That blocks AgentV from being a live observability product.

A full architecture + 3-MVP plan is in the wiki synthesis:
**tsoyang-org/ai-research-wiki#11** → `concepts/agentv-realtime-observability.md`

This issue tracks MVP-2 (`agentv watch`) from that plan. MVP-3 (in-flight scoring) gets a separate followup once MVP-2 ships.

## Proposal: `agentv watch <target>`

Reuse the existing `OtelStreamingObserver` around a free-form agent invocation:

```bash
agentv watch claude-sdk -i prompt.md
agentv watch claude-sdk -i prompt.md --collect          # also write .agentv/traces/watch-<ts>.otlp.json
echo "do the thing" | agentv watch codex --otel-endpoint http://localhost:4317
```

Behavior:
- No test case, no `evaluate()`, no graders.
- Same provider adapters as `agentv run`; same streaming observer; same `gen_ai.*` span attributes.
- Subagent trace propagation: set `TRACEPARENT` + `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` in the child process env so anything reading W3C tracecontext (Copilot CLI, claude-agent-sdk, nested `agentv watch`) becomes a child span automatically.
- Output: stdout (agent response) + OTLP stream to whatever `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` says. Optional `--collect` writes OTLP JSON to `.agentv/traces/` for offline replay.

Implementation surface: `apps/cli/src/commands/watch/` consuming `packages/core/src/observability/` directly. No new wire format. No new exporters.

## Why this slice, why now

- **Smallest blast radius.** The streaming observer and exporter already exist; this command wires them up outside the orchestrator. No new infrastructure.
- **Unlocks MVP-3.** Once `agentv watch` exists, in-flight scoring is just adding grader hooks at span-close in the watch command.
- **Unlocks harness-quality eval.** A `--collect` flag over real Claude/Codex/Copilot sessions is exactly the input shape needed for edit-survival and longitudinal-evaluation work (see VS Code Copilot's `four_gram` / `no_revert` precedent in [[vscode-copilot-agents]]).
- **No coupling to a backend.** Aspire Dashboard, Phoenix, Langfuse, Braintrust, Datadog — all consume OTLP. AgentV stays the emitter.

## Acceptance criteria

- `agentv watch claude-sdk -i prompt.md` produces a complete OTel trace tree (root `invoke_agent` span + `chat` + `execute_tool` children) visible in any OTLP backend.
- `agentv watch <target> --collect` writes a valid OTLP-JSON file under `.agentv/traces/` that any OTel backend (e.g. `otel-cli`) can ingest.
- Subagent spans nest correctly when AgentV is invoked from another AgentV-instrumented process (verify via local Aspire Dashboard run — `docker run mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspire-dashboard`).
- Docs page at `apps/web/src/content/docs/observability/watch.md` plus the 6-line Aspire `docker run` recipe for the "first 5 minutes" path.
- Unit tests for the watch command match the `OtelStreamingObserver` test patterns already in `packages/core/test/observability/`.

## Non-goals (deferred)

- **In-flight scoring** — separate followup issue (MVP-3 in the synthesis); needs span-close hooks + sampling story.
- **Hosted dashboard.** Aspire / Phoenix / Langfuse already exist. The product-strategy in [[agentv-improvement-opportunities]] §"Out-of-scope" is explicit.
- **New wire format.** OTel + OTLP is the format.

## References

- Wiki synthesis (PR): tsoyang-org/ai-research-wiki#11
- Wiki page: `concepts/agentv-realtime-observability.md`
- Existing code: `packages/core/src/observability/otel-exporter.ts`, `apps/cli/src/commands/eval/run-eval.ts:843`
- Closed prerequisites: #298 #299 #300 #301 #302 #304 #305 #306","labels":[],"number":1263,"title":"RFC: agentv watch — non-eval live observability surface","url":"https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv/issues/1263"}

payload.arguments:
{"cmd":"gh issue edit 1263 --repo EntityProcess/agentv --title "feat(cli): prepare AgentV workspaces for manual or external-agent attempts" --body-file - <<'EOF'
## Decision

Do **not** build `agentv watch`.

The useful part of the original request is not a new live-observability runner. The useful primitive is: **materialize the same AgentV eval workspace and task prompt so a human or external agent can attempt the case manually, then let AgentV grade the resulting workspace and optional trace later.**

Live tracing should be configured in the harness or agent itself, for example through Opik/Phoenix/OTel env vars, target hooks, or provider-native config. AgentV can import or grade those traces after the fact through the normalized trajectory/import work. AgentV should not become a parallel live observability product surface.

## Revised scope: `agentv prepare`

Add a CLI command that prepares one eval case for a manual or external-agent attempt:

```bash
agentv prepare evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --target codex --out /tmp/agentv-case-1
```

Expected output directory:

```text
/tmp/agentv-case-1/
  workspace/              # materialized repo/template/hooks applied
  prompt.md               # task prompt to give the human or external agent
  agentv_prepare.json     # snake_case manifest with eval/test/target/workspace metadata
```

`agentv prepare` should:

- Resolve the eval file, selected test case, and target.
- Materialize the workspace using existing AgentV primitives: template copy, `workspace.repos`, Docker/static/pooled/temp behavior where applicable, and lifecycle hooks.
- Run setup only: workspace `before_all`, target `before_all`, workspace `before_each`, target `before_each`.
- Generate a task prompt suitable for the human or external agent.
- Write an auditable manifest with repo pins, selected test, target, hook order, prompt path, workspace path, and created timestamp.
- Print the workspace path and next-step command hints.

`agentv prepare` must not:

- Launch the agent.
- Run graders.
- Claim an eval run completed.
- Emit Opik/Phoenix/OTel traces directly.
- Expose hidden tests, expected output, grader internals, or rubrics in `prompt.md` unless explicitly allowed by eval config.

## Follow-up: grade a prepared attempt

After a human or external agent works in the prepared workspace, AgentV should be able to grade the final state without re-running the target:

```bash
agentv grade evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 --prepared /tmp/agentv-case-1
```

If trajectory matters and an external trace exists, grade with an imported trace:

```bash
agentv grade evals/foo.eval.yaml --test-id case-1 \
  --prepared /tmp/agentv-case-1 \
  --trace opik:<project>/<trace-id>
```

The trace path should route through AgentV's normalized trajectory import/projection work. Provider-native session logs, Opik traces, and OTLP files should be adapters into AgentV's trajectory model; graders should not consume vendor-specific trace shapes directly.

## Product boundary

This replaces the old `agentv watch` proposal:

- Workspace preparation is an AgentV primitive.
- Live observability belongs in Opik/Phoenix/OTel or provider-native instrumentation.
- AgentV remains the source of truth for eval definitions, workspace setup, grading, result bundles, and CI gates.
- Opik/Phoenix traces are optional input artifacts for later grading, not a reason to add a live non-eval runner.

## Implementation plan

1. Add `agentv prepare` for a single eval/test/target.
2. Reuse existing workspace materialization and hook execution instead of duplicating setup logic.
3. Define and write `agentv_prepare.json` with snake_case wire keys.
4. Generate `prompt.md` from the eval input and safe user-facing task context.
5. Add `agentv grade --prepared` or an equivalent prepared-attempt grading command that reuses existing graders against the final workspace state without running the target.
6. Wire optional trace input through normalized trajectory import work; start with local trace/session files if Opik direct fetch depends on existing export/import infrastructure.
7. Document the workflow: prepare workspace → human/external agent works → grade prepared attempt → optionally import/grade traces.

## Acceptance criteria

- `agentv prepare <eval> --test-id <id> --target <target> --out <dir>` creates a prepared workspace with `workspace/`, `prompt.md`, and `agentv_prepare.json`.
- The prepared workspace matches the setup state an AgentV eval run would give the selected target before target execution.
- Setup uses existing workspace/repo/hook primitives and respects target hooks.
- `prompt.md` contains the task input and safe execution instructions, but not hidden expected outputs or grader internals.
- The manifest uses `snake_case` and is sufficient to audit and later grade the attempt.
- A prepared attempt can be graded without re-running the agent target.
- Optional trace/session input can enrich trajectory-based graders through the normalized trace import path.
- Docs explain how to configure Opik/Phoenix/OTel in the harness or target hooks instead of using `agentv watch`.

## Non-goals

- No `agentv watch` command.
- No AgentV-hosted live observability product.
- No built-in Opik/Phoenix tracing runner inside `prepare`.
- No hidden-test or oracle leakage into manual prompts.
- No new benchmark-family-specific workspace schema.

## References

- Research wiki: `concepts/benchmark-provenance-workspace-patterns.md`
- Research wiki: `concepts/production-llm-evaluation-observability-loop.md`
- Research wiki: `concepts/harness-quality-evaluation.md`
- Existing AgentV workspace docs: `apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/guides/workspace-architecture.mdx`

payload.arguments: {"cmd":"bd create \"cli: grade prepared workspace attempts without rerunning targets\" --type feature --priority P1 --parent av-44n --deps \"blocks:av-44n.2\" --labels \"cli,graders,workspace,manual-attempts,results\" --description \"Add the prepared-attempt grading path after a human or external agent has modified a prepared workspace. AgentV should run graders against the final workspace state and write a normal result artifact without launching the agent target again.\" --design \"Prefer extending the existing eval/results pipeline with an explicit prepared-attempt mode over creating a parallel grading engine. Input shape can be agentv grade <eval> --test-id <id> --prepared <dir> or an equivalent existing command if the CLI already has a better home. The prepared manifest should supply workspace path, prompt path, baseline/setup metadata, and selected target. File-change/code graders should use the final workspace. Trajectory graders should be skipped or marked unavailable unless a trace/session input is provided.\" --acceptance \"A prepared workspace can be graded after manual edits without invoking the target provider. Existing deterministic/code/LLM graders that only need input/output/file_changes/workspace_path run with correct payloads. Result output is a normal AgentV run workspace/index.jsonl with explicit metadata indicating prepared/manual source. Missing or invalid agentv_prepare.json fails clearly. Tests cover successful grading, missing manifest, workspace diff capture, and trajectory-grader behavior when no trace is supplied.\" --json","workdir":"/home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv","yield_time_ms":30000,"max_output_tokens":20000}

payload.arguments: {"cmd":"bd create \"trace: enrich prepared-attempt grading from imported Opik or session traces\" --type task --priority P2 --parent av-44n --deps \"blocks:av-44n.3\" --labels \"trace-evaluation,opik,otel,import,graders,manual-attempts\" --description \"Allow prepared-attempt grading to use an optional trace/session artifact produced by the external harness or agent. This is the path for Opik/Phoenix/OTel integration after agentv prepare: the harness emits traces; AgentV imports or references them and runs trajectory-aware graders.\" --design \"Do not add agentv watch and do not make prepare emit traces. Accept trace inputs only as artifacts/references after the attempt. Start with local normalized trajectory or OTLP/session files if direct Opik fetching depends on av-vwa.16.* export/import work. Opik trace references such as opik:<project>/<trace-id> should resolve through an adapter that maps to AgentV's normalized trajectory model. Graders consume normalized trajectory context only, never vendor-native Opik/Phoenix shapes.\" --acceptance \"Prepared-attempt grading can accept a trace input and pass normalized trajectory context to trajectory-aware graders. Local file input is supported first if remote Opik access is not ready. Direct Opik reference support is either implemented through existing Opik/import infrastructure or explicitly blocked on the relevant trace projection/import Beads. Missing/unavailable trace input produces a clear error or a clear skipped-trajectory result. Docs show configuring Opik/OTel in harness hooks instead of agentv watch.\" --json","workdir":"/home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv","yield_time_ms":30000,"max_output_tokens":20000}

Manage dependencies between issues.

When called with an issue ID and --blocks flag, creates a blocking dependency:
  bd dep <blocker-id> --blocks <blocked-id>

This is equivalent to:
  bd dep add <blocked-id> <blocker-id>

Examples:
  bd dep bd-xyz --blocks bd-abc    # bd-xyz blocks bd-abc
  bd dep add bd-abc bd-xyz         # Same as above (bd-abc depends on bd-xyz)

Usage:
  bd dep [issue-id] [flags]
  bd dep [command]

Available Commands:
  add         Add a dependency
  cycles      Detect dependency cycles

  relate      Create a bidirectional relates_to link between issues
  remove      Remove a dependency
  tree        Show dependency tree
  unrelate    Remove a relates_to link between issues
